session.title: B/B Core Question Bank
session.disclaimer: AI-generated OpenMCAT practice. Verify explanations when uncertain.

passages.title: Allosteric regulation of a glycolytic kinase
passages.text: Researchers studied PFK-X, a fictional tetrameric phosphofructokinase isoform that catalyzes the conversion of fructose 6-phosphate (F6P) and ATP to fructose 1,6-bisphosphate and ADP in rapidly dividing cells. ATP is both a substrate and, at high concentration, a negative allosteric regulator. AMP and fructose 2,6-bisphosphate favor the active R state, whereas citrate favors the less active T state. The investigators purified wild-type PFK-X and several variants, then measured initial velocity at pH 7.4 while varying F6P. ATP was held at 2.0 mM in all assays.

tables.caption: Kinetic properties of PFK-X under selected conditions.
Oligomeric state of PFK-X variants measured by native gel electrophoresis.

figureDescriptions.caption: Initial velocity curves for PFK-X.
figureDescriptions.description: Wild-type PFK-X shows a sigmoidal dependence on F6P. Addition of AMP shifts the curve left with little change in the plateau. Addition of citrate shifts the curve right. The R212E variant shows a more hyperbolic curve that changes minimally when AMP is added.

passages.title: Electron transport, proton coupling, and cytochrome c release
passages.text: A fictional study examined isolated mitochondria from cultured liver-derived cells. Investigators supplied either pyruvate plus malate, which generate NADH for Complex I, or succinate, which donates electrons through Complex II. They then added inhibitors or a pore-forming BAX-derived peptide. Oxygen consumption, ATP synthesis, inner mitochondrial membrane potential, and cytochrome c detected outside mitochondria were measured. Values are normalized to untreated mitochondria supplied with pyruvate plus malate unless otherwise noted.

figureDescriptions.caption: Simplified map of oxidative phosphorylation.
figureDescriptions.description: Electrons from NADH enter at Complex I, electrons from succinate enter at Complex II, both pathways pass through ubiquinone to Complex III, cytochrome c, and Complex IV. Complexes I, III, and IV pump protons to create the proton-motive force used by ATP synthase.

passages.title: Regulation of a bacterial nucleotide-reduction operon
passages.text: A fictional bacterial operon, nrdPQ, encodes proteins required for deoxyribonucleotide synthesis during low-oxygen growth. The operon contains a sigma-factor promoter, a 5' untranslated region with a metabolite M-sensitive attenuator, and a Shine-Dalgarno sequence upstream of the nrdP start codon. When metabolite M binds the nascent 5' untranslated region, a terminator hairpin forms before the coding sequence, reducing production of full-length mRNA. Researchers constructed mutations and measured full-length nrdP mRNA, NrdP protein, and NrdP enzyme activity.

tables.caption: Expression and activity of nrdP constructs.
rows: Active-site Cys to Ser mutant, no M

figureDescriptions.caption: Model of metabolite M-dependent attenuation.
figureDescriptions.description: In the presence of metabolite M, the 5' untranslated region folds into a terminator hairpin that causes RNA polymerase to stop before transcribing the nrdP coding sequence. When M is absent or the hairpin is disrupted, RNA polymerase usually produces full-length mRNA.

passages.title: Pigment inheritance and enzyme oligomerization in a model yeast
passages.text: A fictional yeast synthesizes a purple pigment in two steps. Enzyme A converts a colorless precursor to a blue intermediate, and enzyme B converts the blue intermediate to the purple product. Loss of enzyme A prevents blue intermediate formation and produces white colonies regardless of enzyme B genotype. Loss of enzyme B in a strain with functional enzyme A produces blue colonies. The genes A and B are autosomal and are hypothesized to assort independently. Researchers crossed AABB yeast to aabb yeast, then selfed the AaBb F1 progeny.

tables.caption: Observed F2 colony phenotypes from AaBb selfing.
Biochemical characterization of enzyme B variants.

figureDescriptions.caption: Native gel analysis of enzyme B.
figureDescriptions.description: Wild-type enzyme B migrates mainly as a dimer. The Gly45Asp variant shows a stronger monomer band and weaker dimer band. Adding a stabilizing osmolyte partially restores the dimer band.

passages.title: Pentose phosphate pathway flux during oxidative stress
passages.text: A fictional erythroid cell line lacks mitochondria after differentiation and relies on glycolysis for ATP. It also uses the oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) to generate NADPH for reduction of glutathione. Researchers compared wild-type cells with cells expressing a mutant glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD-M). They exposed cells to an oxidant and measured redox status, lactate release, and radiolabeled carbon dioxide production from glucose labeled at carbon 1 or carbon 6. In the oxidative PPP, carbon 1 of glucose 6-phosphate is released as CO2 while NADPH is produced.

figureDescriptions.caption: Relationship between glycolysis and the oxidative PPP.
figureDescriptions.description: Glucose 6-phosphate can enter glycolysis or the oxidative PPP. The oxidative PPP produces NADPH and ribulose 5-phosphate, releasing carbon 1 as CO2. Glycolysis in these differentiated erythroid cells ends mainly in lactate formation.

passages.title: Kinetic analysis of a cytosolic lactate dehydrogenase variant
passages.text: Researchers studied LDH-R, a fictional homotetrameric cytosolic lactate dehydrogenase that catalyzes the reversible reaction pyruvate + NADH + H+ -> lactate + NAD+. LDH-R helps maintain cytosolic redox balance when oxidative metabolism is limited. The investigators purified wild-type LDH-R, two point variants, and one truncation variant. Initial velocity was measured at saturating NADH unless NADH affinity was being assessed. PYA is a pyruvate analog that does not undergo reduction.

tables.caption: Properties of LDH-R proteins under selected assay conditions.

figureDescriptions.caption: Initial velocity curves for LDH-R.
figureDescriptions.description: At saturating NADH, wild-type LDH-R shows a hyperbolic increase in velocity with increasing pyruvate. PYA shifts the wild-type curve to the right without lowering the plateau. H177Q has a very low plateau. L246D has a lower plateau than wild type. At pH 6.6, the wild-type curve shifts slightly left and reaches a higher plateau.

passages.title: Alternative splicing and protein dimerization of LUM1
passages.text: A fictional eukaryotic gene, LUM1, contains four exons. Exon 3 encodes a hydrophobic alpha-helical segment required for stable LUM1 dimer formation. A regulatory RNA-binding protein, RBP-K, binds an intronic splicing enhancer near exon 3 and promotes exon 3 inclusion. Skipping exon 3 preserves the reading frame but produces a mostly monomeric protein that is degraded more rapidly. A separate mutation in the 5' splice donor of intron 2 causes intron retention and introduces a premature termination codon more than 50 nucleotides upstream of the final exon-exon junction.

tables.caption: Effects of LUM1 expression constructs in cultured cells.

figureDescriptions.caption: Model for LUM1 isoform production.
figureDescriptions.description: When exon 3 is included, LUM1 contains a hydrophobic helical segment that supports dimerization. When exon 3 is skipped, the reading frame remains intact but the helical dimerization segment is absent. Intron 2 retention creates a premature stop codon predicted to trigger nonsense-mediated mRNA decay.

passages.title: Fidelity of a threonyl-tRNA synthetase
passages.text: A fictional threonyl-tRNA synthetase, ThrRS-F, attaches threonine to tRNAThr during translation. The first active site activates threonine by forming threonyl-AMP from threonine and ATP; the amino acid is then transferred to the 3' end of tRNAThr. Because valine is structurally similar to threonine, ThrRS-F also contains an editing domain that hydrolyzes mischarged Val-tRNAThr. A reporter protein loses activity when valine is inserted at positions normally encoded by threonine codons.

tables.caption: Aminoacylation and editing properties of ThrRS-F.

figureDescriptions.caption: Two-site model of ThrRS-F specificity.
figureDescriptions.description: The activation site forms aminoacyl-AMP intermediates using ATP. Correct threonylated tRNA is released for translation. Incorrect Val-tRNAThr can enter an editing domain, where the aminoacyl ester bond is hydrolyzed before the tRNA reaches the ribosome.

passages.title: Nuclear genes required for Complex IV assembly in a model alga
passages.text: A fictional unicellular alga uses nuclear genes E and F to assemble functional mitochondrial Complex IV. Gene E encodes a copper-delivery chaperone, and gene F encodes a Complex IV structural subunit. Loss of either gene produces a slow-respiration phenotype, and loss of both genes is not more severe than loss of either single gene. The genes are autosomal and assort independently. Researchers crossed EEFF algae with eeff algae, selfed the EeFf F1, and measured F2 respiration phenotypes. Biochemical assays were performed on representative genotypes.

tables.caption: F2 respiration phenotypes after EeFf selfing.

figureDescriptions.caption: Electron flow to Complex IV.
figureDescriptions.description: Electrons from NADH enter through Complex I and are transferred through ubiquinone, Complex III, cytochrome c, and Complex IV. Complex IV transfers electrons to oxygen. When Complex IV activity is low, upstream electron carriers become more reduced and oxygen consumption falls.

passages.title: Gluconeogenic flux in hepatocyte-like cells
passages.text: Researchers used fictional hepatocyte-like HGL cells to study glucose production from lactate and alanine. Pyruvate generated in the cytosol must enter mitochondria through the mitochondrial pyruvate carrier (MPC). In the mitochondrial matrix, pyruvate carboxylase (PC) uses ATP, bicarbonate, and a biotin cofactor to convert pyruvate to oxaloacetate. Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) then uses GTP to convert oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate. The pathway requires substantial energy input and is sensitive to cellular ATP availability.

tables.caption: Metabolic effects of perturbing gluconeogenesis from lactate and alanine.

figureDescriptions.caption: Simplified route from lactate and alanine to glucose.
figureDescriptions.description: Lactate and alanine are converted to cytosolic pyruvate. Pyruvate enters mitochondria through MPC and is carboxylated by PC to oxaloacetate. Oxaloacetate-derived carbon returns to the cytosol and proceeds through gluconeogenic reactions to form glucose.

questions.stem: An investigator synthesizes the capped peptide Ac-Asp-His-Lys-NH2. At pH 5.5, which net charge is expected if the side-chain pKa values are approximately Asp 3.9, His 6.0, and Lys 10.5?
questions.explanation: At pH 5.5, the acetylated N-terminus and amidated C-terminus do not contribute charge. Asp is mostly deprotonated (-1), His is mostly protonated (+1), and Lys is protonated (+1), giving a net charge of +1.

choiceExplanations.A: This ignores the protonation of His at pH 5.5 or treats Lys and Asp as exactly canceling all side-chain charge.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly sums Asp (-1), His (+1), and Lys (+1) while recognizing that the termini are blocked.
choiceExplanations.C: This would be correct only if Asp were neutral or if an additional positive terminus were present.
choiceExplanations.D: This treats the basic side chains as neutral or deprotonated at a pH below their pKa values.

questions.commonMistake: Counting free terminal charges even though the peptide is acetylated and amidated.

questions.stem: A purified enzyme loses activity in 8 M urea but regains most activity after slow dialysis into physiological buffer. Which statement best explains this observation?

choices.text: Urea hydrolyzed peptide bonds, and dialysis allowed the original primary structure to reform.
The enzyme's amino acid sequence retained enough information to refold when denaturant was removed.
Urea irreversibly reduced all peptide bonds, but dialysis restored the active-site pH.
The enzyme must be a ribozyme because proteins cannot regain activity after denaturation.

questions.explanation: Urea disrupts noncovalent interactions that stabilize secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure, but it does not normally cleave peptide bonds. If primary structure remains intact, some proteins can refold after denaturant removal.

choiceExplanations.A: Urea does not normally hydrolyze peptide bonds under these conditions, and peptide-bond reformation would require biosynthesis or chemical ligation.
choiceExplanations.B: This reflects the principle that primary structure can encode folding information when covalent structure is preserved.
choiceExplanations.C: Peptide bonds are not reduced by urea, and pH alone would not restore an irreversibly damaged primary structure.
choiceExplanations.D: Proteins can sometimes refold after denaturation; the observation does not imply an RNA catalyst.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming any loss of activity means covalent cleavage of the protein.

questions.stem: An inhibitor of a Michaelis-Menten enzyme increases the apparent Km from 4 micromolar to 12 micromolar but does not change Vmax when substrate is saturating. Which mechanism is most consistent with these data?

choices.text: Competitive inhibition at or near the active site
Pure noncompetitive inhibition at a separate site
Irreversible covalent inactivation of half the enzyme molecules
Uncompetitive inhibition that binds only the enzyme-substrate complex

questions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors reduce apparent substrate affinity, increasing apparent Km, but their effect can be overcome by sufficiently high substrate concentration, so Vmax is unchanged.

choiceExplanations.A: This matches the increase in apparent Km with unchanged Vmax.
choiceExplanations.B: Pure noncompetitive inhibition decreases Vmax without changing Km in the simple model.
choiceExplanations.C: Irreversible loss of active enzyme would lower Vmax.
choiceExplanations.D: Uncompetitive inhibition lowers both apparent Km and Vmax.

questions.commonMistake: Reversing how competitive and noncompetitive inhibitors affect Km and Vmax.

questions.stem: A double-stranded DNA fragment contains 18% cytosine. Assuming normal Watson-Crick base pairing, what percentage of the fragment is adenine?
questions.explanation: In double-stranded DNA, cytosine pairs with guanine, so G is also 18%. The remaining 64% must be A plus T, and A equals T, so adenine is 32%.

choiceExplanations.A: This incorrectly pairs adenine with cytosine or assumes all bases have equal percentages.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies Chargaff's rules: C = G = 18%, leaving A = T = 32%.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the combined percentage of cytosine plus guanine, not adenine.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the combined percentage of adenine plus thymine.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting that the remaining percentage must be divided equally between A and T.

questions.stem: A biosynthetic reaction has Delta G = +12.4 kJ/mol under cellular conditions. If it is directly coupled to ATP hydrolysis with Delta G = -30.5 kJ/mol under the same conditions, what is the best prediction for the coupled process?

choices.text: It is unfavorable because one step has a positive Delta G.
It is favorable with a net Delta G of -18.1 kJ/mol.
It is at equilibrium because ATP hydrolysis cancels the biosynthetic reaction exactly.
It is favorable only if ATP is regenerated in the same enzyme active site.

questions.explanation: Free energy changes for coupled reactions are additive. The total Delta G is +12.4 + (-30.5) = -18.1 kJ/mol, so the coupled process is thermodynamically favorable.

choiceExplanations.A: A positive Delta G step can proceed when coupled to a sufficiently favorable step.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly adds the free energy changes and interprets the negative value.
choiceExplanations.C: The values do not cancel; the net free energy change is negative.
choiceExplanations.D: ATP regeneration is not required in the same active site for coupling to be favorable.

questions.commonMistake: Treating the sign of one component reaction as determining the whole coupled process.

questions.stem: Based on Table p1_t1, which conclusion best describes the effect of AMP on wild-type PFK-X?

choices.text: AMP converts PFK-X from a tetramer to a monomer.
AMP competitively inhibits F6P binding at the active site.
AMP causes irreversible inactivation of a large fraction of PFK-X.
AMP increases the apparent affinity for F6P with little effect on maximal catalytic capacity.

questions.explanation: AMP lowers the S0.5 for F6P from 0.80 mM to 0.25 mM while leaving relative Vmax nearly unchanged. This pattern is consistent with allosteric activation that increases the fraction of enzyme in a high-affinity active state.

choiceExplanations.A: The passage describes PFK-X as tetrameric, and no AMP-dependent monomerization data are provided.
choiceExplanations.B: Competitive inhibition would increase the apparent substrate concentration required for activity, not decrease it.
choiceExplanations.C: Irreversible inactivation would be expected to lower Vmax substantially.
choiceExplanations.D: This matches the decreased S0.5 and nearly unchanged Vmax.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting a left-shifted velocity curve as inhibition rather than activation.

questions.stem: The R212E variant remains mostly tetrameric but responds minimally to AMP. Which interpretation is most directly supported?

choices.text: R212E primarily blocks ATP binding at the catalytic site.
R212E disrupts an AMP-dependent regulatory interaction or conformational response.
R212E prevents all protein folding into stable secondary structure.
R212E increases citrate activation of the enzyme.

questions.explanation: R212E has a lower Hill coefficient and little response to AMP, yet Table p1_t2 shows it remains mostly tetrameric. The best supported interpretation is a defect in AMP-mediated allosteric regulation rather than gross oligomer loss or catalytic ATP binding.

choiceExplanations.A: The data do not show ATP binding directly, and the enzyme retains substantial activity.
choiceExplanations.B: This accounts for preserved tetramer formation with loss of AMP responsiveness.
choiceExplanations.C: A protein lacking stable secondary structure would not be expected to remain mostly tetrameric and active.
choiceExplanations.D: Citrate activation is not shown; citrate is described as a negative regulator of wild-type PFK-X.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming any regulatory mutation must destroy the active site or the entire quaternary structure.

questions.stem: If initial velocity were compared at 0.20 mM F6P and 2.0 mM ATP, which condition should produce the highest rate among the choices below?

choices.text: Wild type + citrate
Wild type + AMP
R212E variant + AMP
D150N variant

questions.explanation: At low F6P, the enzyme condition with the lowest S0.5 should reach a larger fraction of its Vmax. Wild type plus AMP has the lowest S0.5 in Table p1_t1 and a normal Vmax, so it should have the highest rate at 0.20 mM F6P.

choiceExplanations.A: Citrate raises S0.5, so low F6P would poorly activate the enzyme.
choiceExplanations.B: This condition has the lowest S0.5 and nearly full Vmax, favoring activity at low F6P.
choiceExplanations.C: R212E plus AMP has a much higher S0.5 than wild type plus AMP and a lower Vmax.
choiceExplanations.D: D150N has less than 5% relative Vmax, so it would not have the highest rate.

questions.commonMistake: Comparing only Vmax while ignoring the substrate concentration used in the proposed assay.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best distinguish whether R212E eliminates AMP binding itself or instead permits AMP binding but blocks the allosteric conformational response?

choices.text: Run SDS-PAGE after boiling all PFK-X samples in detergent.
Measure total cellular ATP after overexpressing wild-type PFK-X in intact cells.
Measure AMP binding to purified wild-type and R212E PFK-X, then compare activity changes in the same preparations.
Determine whether citrate changes the absorbance of AMP at 260 nm.

questions.explanation: Directly measuring AMP binding and coupling that result to activity would distinguish loss of ligand binding from loss of downstream allosteric communication. If R212E binds AMP normally but is not activated, the mutation likely affects conformational coupling rather than binding.

choiceExplanations.A: Denaturing SDS-PAGE would assess subunit size, not AMP binding or allosteric signaling.
choiceExplanations.B: Cellular ATP is indirect and would not isolate AMP binding by R212E.
choiceExplanations.C: This design directly tests both ligand binding and functional response.
choiceExplanations.D: Citrate effects on AMP absorbance would not test the mutant enzyme's AMP binding or response.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing an indirect cellular readout when the mechanistic alternatives concern purified ligand binding and enzyme response.

questions.stem: D150N PFK-X is mostly tetrameric but has less than 5% relative Vmax. Which explanation is most consistent with the substitution and the data?

choices.text: D150N increases the Hill coefficient beyond the assay's detection range.
Asp150 is required only for tetramer assembly.
Asp150 is likely important for catalysis or substrate positioning in the active site.
D150N makes AMP a covalent substrate of PFK-X.

questions.explanation: Asp to Asn preserves approximate side-chain size but removes a carboxylate charge. Because D150N remains mostly tetrameric while activity is nearly abolished, Asp150 is most likely important for catalytic chemistry or active-site electrostatics rather than quaternary assembly.

choiceExplanations.A: A high Hill coefficient would not by itself explain Vmax below 5%.
choiceExplanations.B: Table p1_t2 shows D150N is 90% tetrameric, arguing against a primary tetramer assembly defect.
choiceExplanations.C: This connects the conservative size change but loss of charge to an active-site functional defect.
choiceExplanations.D: No evidence suggests AMP becomes a covalent substrate.

questions.commonMistake: Equating loss of enzyme activity with loss of oligomerization despite the native gel data.

questions.stem: An mRNA segment is read by a ribosome as 5'-AUG GAA UGC UAA-3'. For the codon GAA, which tRNA anticodon is correctly written in the antiparallel orientation?
questions.explanation: Codon-anticodon pairing is antiparallel and complementary. The mRNA codon 5'-GAA-3' pairs with the tRNA anticodon 3'-CUU-5'.

choiceExplanations.A: This is identical to the codon, not complementary.
choiceExplanations.B: This is complementary but written parallel rather than antiparallel to the codon as asked.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the complementary anticodon written 3' to 5' opposite the codon.
choiceExplanations.D: This has the wrong base order for an anticodon paired to 5'-GAA-3'.

questions.commonMistake: Writing a complementary sequence without checking the requested 5' or 3' orientation.

questions.stem: In a testcross, an organism with genotype AaBb is crossed to aabb. The A and B genes assort independently, and both dominant alleles are completely dominant. What fraction of offspring are expected to show both dominant phenotypes?
questions.explanation: The AaBb parent produces AB, Ab, aB, and ab gametes in equal proportions. Only the AB gamete paired with ab from the tester produces AaBb offspring showing both dominant phenotypes, so the expected fraction is 1/4.

choiceExplanations.A: This would fit a two-gene event in an F2 self-cross, not this testcross.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly uses equal gamete frequencies from the heterozygous parent.
choiceExplanations.C: This counts offspring with either dominant phenotype, not both.
choiceExplanations.D: This is too high for a testcross requiring both dominant alleles from one heterozygous parent.

questions.commonMistake: Applying a 9:3:3:1 F2 pattern to a testcross.

questions.stem: During intense anaerobic glycolysis, pyruvate is reduced to lactate. What is the most important purpose of this reaction for continued ATP production by glycolysis?

choices.text: It generates FADH2 for substrate-level phosphorylation.
It regenerates NAD+ needed by glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase.
It converts ATP into ADP for phosphofructokinase-1.
It releases CO2 to make pyruvate oxidation favorable.

questions.explanation: Lactate fermentation oxidizes NADH to NAD+. NAD+ is required for the glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase step of glycolysis, allowing glycolysis to continue producing ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation.

choiceExplanations.A: FADH2 is not generated by lactate fermentation.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly identifies NAD+ regeneration as the key role of lactate formation.
choiceExplanations.C: Fermentation does not primarily regulate glycolysis by consuming ATP.
choiceExplanations.D: Lactate fermentation does not release CO2; pyruvate decarboxylation is a different process.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing lactate fermentation with oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate.

questions.stem: A compound makes the inner mitochondrial membrane permeable to protons but does not block electron transfer. Which immediate pattern is most likely in mitochondria supplied with NADH-generating substrates?

choices.text: Oxygen consumption decreases and ATP synthesis increases.
Oxygen consumption increases and ATP synthesis decreases.
Both oxygen consumption and ATP synthesis decrease because NADH cannot be oxidized.
Both oxygen consumption and ATP synthesis increase because the proton gradient is larger.

questions.explanation: A protonophore uncouples electron transport from ATP synthesis by dissipating the proton gradient. Electron transport can accelerate because backpressure from the gradient is relieved, but ATP synthase cannot efficiently use the gradient to make ATP.

choiceExplanations.A: This reverses the typical effect of uncoupling.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the expected result of dissipating the proton-motive force without blocking electron transfer.
choiceExplanations.C: NADH oxidation can continue when electron transfer is intact.
choiceExplanations.D: The proton gradient is dissipated, not enlarged.

questions.commonMistake: Treating uncoupling as the same as inhibition of the electron transport chain.

questions.stem: A point mutation disrupts the AAUAAA polyadenylation signal in a eukaryotic pre-mRNA without changing the coding sequence. Which outcome is most likely?

choices.text: Defective 3' end processing and reduced mature mRNA stability
Complete failure of codon-anticodon pairing at the ribosome
Replacement of thymine with uracil in genomic DNA
Loss of peptide bond formation by rRNA

questions.explanation: The AAUAAA signal helps direct cleavage and polyadenylation of eukaryotic pre-mRNA. Disrupting it can impair 3' end formation and reduce stability or export of the mature mRNA without altering the encoded amino acid sequence directly.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly links the polyadenylation signal to mRNA processing and stability.
choiceExplanations.B: Codon-anticodon pairing depends on the coding sequence and tRNAs, not directly on the polyadenylation signal.
choiceExplanations.C: DNA contains thymine; a pre-mRNA processing signal mutation does not replace genomic thymine with uracil.
choiceExplanations.D: Peptide bond formation is catalyzed by ribosomal RNA and is not specifically lost due to this 3' processing signal mutation.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming any mRNA mutation must change the protein's amino acid sequence.

questions.stem: Which site of inhibition best explains the rotenone results in Table p2_t1?

choices.text: ATP synthase
Complex II
Complex I
Cytochrome c release pore

questions.explanation: Rotenone strongly lowers oxygen consumption with pyruvate/malate, which supply electrons to Complex I, but succinate largely restores oxygen consumption by entering through Complex II. This pattern localizes the block to Complex I.

choiceExplanations.A: ATP synthase inhibition would not be bypassed by changing electron entry from Complex I to Complex II.
choiceExplanations.B: A Complex II inhibitor would be expected to impair succinate-supported respiration more strongly.
choiceExplanations.C: This matches the substrate-specific rescue by succinate.
choiceExplanations.D: Cytochrome c release is not increased with rotenone in the table.

questions.commonMistake: Failing to use the difference between NADH-linked and succinate-linked substrates.

questions.stem: Oligomycin produces a high membrane potential but low ATP synthesis and reduced oxygen consumption. Which explanation best accounts for this pattern?

choices.text: It blocks Complex IV, preventing oxygen from accepting electrons.
It blocks ATP synthase, causing the proton gradient to build and slowing electron transport.
It carries protons across the membrane, eliminating the proton gradient.
It releases cytochrome c from the intermembrane space.

questions.explanation: Oligomycin inhibits ATP synthase. Protons cannot flow efficiently back through ATP synthase, so membrane potential rises; the accumulated gradient makes further proton pumping and electron transport less favorable, lowering oxygen consumption.

choiceExplanations.A: A Complex IV inhibitor would lower membrane potential rather than raise it as proton pumping collapses.
choiceExplanations.B: This explains high membrane potential, low ATP synthesis, and lower oxygen consumption together.
choiceExplanations.C: A proton carrier would lower membrane potential and often increase oxygen consumption, resembling CCCP.
choiceExplanations.D: The table shows no increase in cytochrome c outside mitochondria with oligomycin.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing ATP synthase inhibition with protonophore-mediated uncoupling.

questions.stem: CCCP causes high oxygen consumption but low ATP synthesis. Which conclusion is most directly supported by this result?

choices.text: Electron transport and ATP synthesis can be experimentally uncoupled by dissipating the proton-motive force.
ATP synthase directly transfers electrons to oxygen during oxidative phosphorylation.
The proton gradient is unnecessary for mitochondrial ATP synthesis.
Complex II is the only respiratory complex that pumps protons.

questions.explanation: CCCP dissipates membrane potential while oxygen consumption rises and ATP synthesis falls. This supports chemiosmotic coupling: electron transport can proceed, but ATP synthesis requires the proton-motive force.

choiceExplanations.A: This is directly supported by high respiration with low ATP synthesis and low membrane potential.
choiceExplanations.B: ATP synthase does not transfer electrons to oxygen; Complex IV reduces oxygen.
choiceExplanations.C: Low ATP synthesis when membrane potential is low shows the gradient is important.
choiceExplanations.D: Complex II does not pump protons, and the figure states that Complexes I, III, and IV do.

questions.commonMistake: Inferring that faster oxygen use must always mean more ATP synthesis.

questions.stem: The BAX-derived peptide increases cytochrome c outside mitochondria. In intact cells, this event would most directly promote which downstream process?

choices.text: Replication of mitochondrial DNA by ribosomes
Conversion of pyruvate to lactate in the cytosol
Charging of tRNAs by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases
Assembly of apoptotic signaling complexes that activate caspases

questions.explanation: Cytochrome c release from the intermembrane space is a key signal in intrinsic apoptosis, promoting apoptosome formation and caspase activation. The passage shows BAX peptide specifically increases cytochrome c outside mitochondria.

choiceExplanations.A: Ribosomes do not replicate DNA, and this is not the pathway indicated by BAX-mediated cytochrome c release.
choiceExplanations.B: Lactate formation is a cytosolic redox reaction not directly triggered by cytochrome c release.
choiceExplanations.C: tRNA charging is part of translation and is not the direct consequence of cytochrome c release.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly connects cytochrome c release to intrinsic apoptotic signaling.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking cytochrome c release primarily increases normal electron transport rather than signaling apoptosis.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the antimycin A data?

choices.text: Blocking Complex III prevents efficient respiration from both Complex I-linked and Complex II-linked electron sources.
Antimycin A selectively blocks only pyruvate import into mitochondria.
Succinate donates electrons downstream of Complex IV.
Antimycin A directly opens the BAX-dependent cytochrome c release pore.

questions.explanation: Antimycin A reduces oxygen consumption to similarly low values with pyruvate/malate and succinate. Because both electron-entry routes converge before Complex III, a Complex III block should impair respiration from either substrate set.

choiceExplanations.A: This conclusion follows from low oxygen consumption with both NADH-linked and succinate-linked substrates.
choiceExplanations.B: If only pyruvate import were blocked, succinate-supported oxygen consumption should remain high.
choiceExplanations.C: Succinate enters through Complex II upstream of ubiquinone and Complex III, not downstream of Complex IV.
choiceExplanations.D: The table shows no increase in cytochrome c outside mitochondria with antimycin A.

questions.commonMistake: Treating the succinate condition as an unrelated control rather than a way to map the inhibited complex.

questions.stem: Most chiral amino acids incorporated into human proteins are L amino acids with S absolute configuration. Which amino acid is the standard exception because its side chain changes the priority order around the alpha carbon?

choices.text: Alanine
Cysteine
Serine
Valine

questions.explanation: Cysteine is an L amino acid but has R absolute configuration because the sulfur-containing side chain receives higher priority under the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog rules than the carboxylate carbon. Glycine is achiral, but among chiral amino acids cysteine is the common exception.

choiceExplanations.A: L-alanine has S configuration at the alpha carbon.
choiceExplanations.B: L-cysteine is the standard chiral exception with R absolute configuration.
choiceExplanations.C: L-serine has S configuration; oxygen in the side chain does not create the same priority exception as cysteine sulfur.
choiceExplanations.D: L-valine has S configuration at the alpha carbon.

questions.commonMistake: Remembering L configuration but assuming it is always identical to S configuration.

questions.stem: An enzyme's velocity curve is sigmoidal rather than hyperbolic when velocity is plotted against substrate concentration. Which interpretation is most likely?

choices.text: The enzyme follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics with no regulatory conformational states.
The enzyme must be a monomer with a single active site.
The enzyme shows positive cooperativity among multiple substrate-binding sites.
The enzyme has been irreversibly denatured and cannot bind substrate.

questions.explanation: A sigmoidal substrate-velocity relationship commonly indicates cooperative binding in an oligomeric or multisite enzyme. Positive cooperativity means binding at one site increases activity or substrate affinity at other sites.

choiceExplanations.A: Simple Michaelis-Menten kinetics produces a hyperbolic, not sigmoidal, relationship.
choiceExplanations.B: A single independent active site typically gives a hyperbolic curve.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the standard interpretation of a sigmoidal saturation curve.
choiceExplanations.D: A denatured enzyme would show little or no organized saturation behavior.

questions.commonMistake: Treating every enzyme saturation curve as simple Michaelis-Menten kinetics.

questions.stem: In a nucleic acid strand, the phosphodiester backbone is formed primarily by linkage of which groups?

choices.text: The 3' hydroxyl of one sugar to the 5' phosphate of the next nucleotide
The 2' hydroxyl of thymine to the amino group of adenine
The 5' base of one nucleotide to the 3' base of the next nucleotide
The phosphate of one base to the peptide bond of the next base

questions.explanation: Nucleic acid polymerization forms phosphodiester bonds between the 3' hydroxyl of the growing strand and the 5' phosphate of the incoming nucleotide. This creates strand directionality from 5' to 3'.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly describes the sugar-phosphate linkage.
choiceExplanations.B: Bases do not form the phosphodiester backbone, and thymine lacks a 2' hydroxyl because it is a base, not a sugar.
choiceExplanations.C: Bases pair by hydrogen bonding but are not connected base-to-base in the backbone.
choiceExplanations.D: Nucleic acids do not contain peptide bonds between bases.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing base pairing with covalent backbone formation.

questions.stem: A leucine residue in the middle of a long alpha helix is replaced by proline. Which effect is most likely?

choices.text: The helix becomes more stable because proline adds an extra backbone N-H hydrogen bond.
The helix is destabilized because proline restricts backbone geometry and lacks a normal backbone N-H donor.
The primary structure is unchanged because leucine and proline are both nonpolar.
The peptide bond adjacent to proline is converted into a phosphodiester bond.

questions.explanation: Proline often disrupts alpha helices because its cyclic side chain restricts backbone phi angles and its backbone nitrogen lacks a hydrogen for normal intrahelical hydrogen bonding. The primary sequence does change because one residue is substituted for another.

choiceExplanations.A: Proline lacks the normal backbone N-H donor and often disrupts, rather than stabilizes, helices.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly explains the common helix-breaking effect of proline.
choiceExplanations.C: Primary structure is the amino acid sequence, so replacing leucine with proline changes it.
choiceExplanations.D: Amino acid substitution does not convert peptide bonds into nucleic acid phosphodiester bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying both residues as hydrophobic and overlooking proline's backbone constraints.

questions.stem: A reaction A -> B has Delta G degrees prime = +3.0 kJ/mol. In a cell, the concentration of B is kept very low relative to A. Which statement best describes the actual Delta G?

choices.text: It must remain +3.0 kJ/mol because standard free energy cannot change.
It can become negative because Delta G depends on the reaction quotient.
It becomes zero whenever enzyme concentration is high.
It becomes more positive because product removal violates conservation of energy.

questions.explanation: Actual free energy is Delta G = Delta G degrees prime + RT ln Q. Keeping product B low relative to reactant A lowers Q, which can make the actual Delta G negative even if Delta G degrees prime is positive.

choiceExplanations.A: Delta G degrees prime is fixed for a given reaction, but actual Delta G depends on concentrations.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies the relationship between free energy and the reaction quotient.
choiceExplanations.C: Enzymes alter rates, not equilibrium free energy.
choiceExplanations.D: Product removal changes chemical potential and does not violate conservation of energy.

questions.commonMistake: Using standard free energy as though it were always equal to cellular free energy.

questions.stem: Which construct in Table p3_t1 most directly identifies a defect in transcription initiation?

choices.text: Terminator hairpin disrupted, +M
Shine-Dalgarno mutant, no M
Active-site Cys to Ser mutant, no M
-10 promoter mutant, no M

questions.explanation: The -10 promoter element is part of the bacterial promoter recognized during transcription initiation. Its mutation lowers full-length mRNA, protein, and activity, consistent with reduced initiation of transcription.

choiceExplanations.A: Disrupting the terminator hairpin increases full-length mRNA in the presence of M, indicating loss of attenuation rather than initiation failure.
choiceExplanations.B: The Shine-Dalgarno mutation leaves mRNA high but protein low, indicating translation initiation impairment.
choiceExplanations.C: The active-site mutation leaves mRNA and protein high but activity low, indicating a catalytic defect.
choiceExplanations.D: This construct alters a promoter sequence and produces low mRNA, matching a transcription initiation defect.

questions.commonMistake: Equating low protein with transcription failure without checking mRNA abundance.

questions.stem: The Shine-Dalgarno mutant produces nearly normal full-length mRNA but little protein. Which process is most directly impaired?

choices.text: DNA replication initiation
Translation initiation
Polyadenylation of eukaryotic mRNA
Termination of the peptide bond by DNA polymerase

questions.explanation: The Shine-Dalgarno sequence helps position bacterial mRNA on the ribosome for translation initiation. Normal mRNA with low protein indicates the transcript is made but poorly translated.

choiceExplanations.A: The construct affects an mRNA translation element, not DNA replication.
choiceExplanations.B: This matches the function of the Shine-Dalgarno sequence and the data pattern.
choiceExplanations.C: The passage describes a bacterial operon, and polyadenylation is not the relevant process here.
choiceExplanations.D: DNA polymerase does not terminate peptide bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting reduced protein abundance as evidence that the mRNA was not transcribed.

questions.stem: What does the terminator hairpin disruption result most strongly support about metabolite M regulation?

choices.text: M reduces nrdP expression mainly by promoting premature transcription termination.
M prevents ribosomes from forming peptide bonds after full-length mRNA is made.
M changes the genetic code so that UAA encodes tryptophan.
M increases nrdP expression by stabilizing the active-site cysteine.

questions.explanation: In wild-type cells, M lowers full-length mRNA and protein. When the terminator hairpin is disrupted, full-length mRNA and protein remain high even with M, supporting a model in which M represses expression by promoting attenuation before the coding region.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the direct implication of high expression when the M-sensitive terminator cannot form.
choiceExplanations.B: If M mainly blocked peptide bond formation after full-length mRNA was made, disrupting a transcription terminator would not restore mRNA and protein so strongly.
choiceExplanations.C: No evidence suggests the genetic code changes.
choiceExplanations.D: M is described as binding the 5' untranslated region, not stabilizing an active-site residue.

questions.commonMistake: Focusing on protein output while ignoring that the key restored measurement is full-length mRNA.

questions.stem: The active-site Cys to Ser mutant has normal mRNA and nearly normal protein but very low activity. Which conclusion is best supported?

choices.text: The mutation causes metabolite M to form the terminator hairpin even when M is absent.
The mutation prevents RNA polymerase from binding the promoter.
The mutation prevents ribosomes from recognizing the Shine-Dalgarno sequence.
The substituted cysteine is important for NrdP catalysis rather than for transcription or translation.

questions.explanation: Because mRNA and protein abundance remain high, transcription and translation are largely intact. The loss of activity therefore most directly indicates that the cysteine side chain contributes to enzyme catalysis or active-site chemistry.

choiceExplanations.A: The data are from no-M conditions and do not indicate metabolite-dependent attenuation.
choiceExplanations.B: A promoter-binding defect would reduce mRNA.
choiceExplanations.C: A Shine-Dalgarno recognition defect would reduce protein despite mRNA production.
choiceExplanations.D: This conclusion follows from normal expression but poor catalytic output.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming every mutation with low activity must reduce protein expression.

questions.stem: Which additional control would best strengthen the conclusion that changes in full-length nrdP mRNA reflect transcriptional regulation rather than unequal RNA recovery between samples?

choices.text: Replace the bacterial promoter with a eukaryotic polyadenylation signal.
Increase metabolite M until all samples have identical enzyme activity.
Measure a stable housekeeping mRNA in each RNA sample and normalize nrdP mRNA to it.
Measure only NrdP protein by Western blot and omit RNA analysis.

questions.explanation: A housekeeping transcript provides a loading and RNA recovery control. Normalizing nrdP mRNA to a stable reference makes it less likely that apparent changes are due to unequal sample input or RNA degradation.

choiceExplanations.A: A polyadenylation signal does not replace bacterial promoter function and would not address sample recovery.
choiceExplanations.B: Forcing enzyme activities to match would obscure the regulatory effect and would not control RNA recovery.
choiceExplanations.C: This directly controls for RNA recovery and loading across samples.
choiceExplanations.D: Protein measurement alone cannot validate whether mRNA differences reflect transcriptional regulation.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing another endpoint measurement rather than a control for the measurement being interpreted.

questions.stem: The end product of a biosynthetic pathway binds reversibly to the first committed enzyme in the pathway. In purified enzyme assays, the end product lowers Vmax with little change in Km for the substrate. Which description best fits this regulation?

choices.text: Cooperative substrate activation with a Hill coefficient greater than 1
Competitive inhibition by the end product at the substrate-binding site
Activation by removal of a covalent phosphate group
Allosteric feedback inhibition that decreases catalytic turnover

questions.explanation: End-product inhibition of the first committed enzyme is feedback regulation. Lower Vmax with little change in Km is consistent with an allosteric effect that reduces catalytic activity rather than direct competition with substrate.

choiceExplanations.A: The data describe inhibition, not cooperative activation by substrate.
choiceExplanations.B: Competitive inhibition would primarily increase apparent Km while leaving Vmax unchanged.
choiceExplanations.C: The stem describes reversible end-product binding, not covalent dephosphorylation.
choiceExplanations.D: This matches feedback regulation and the kinetic pattern.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying any inhibitor as competitive without using the Vmax and Km pattern.

questions.stem: What is the approximate net charge of the tripeptide Lys-Asp-Glu at pH 7.0, assuming free N- and C-termini and standard side-chain pKa values?
questions.explanation: At pH 7, the free N-terminus is +1, the free C-terminus is -1, Lys is +1, Asp is -1, and Glu is -1. The total is +1 -1 +1 -1 -1 = -1.

choiceExplanations.A: This omits at least one acidic group or the C-terminal negative charge.
choiceExplanations.B: This misses one net negative contribution from the acidic side chains plus C-terminus.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly sums the terminal and side-chain charges at pH 7.
choiceExplanations.D: This omits the positive charge on Lys or the N-terminus.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting to include the free N- and C-terminal charges.

questions.stem: A mitochondrial inhibitor blocks Complex IV. Which immediate effect is most likely?

choices.text: Oxygen consumption increases, and NADH becomes more oxidized.
Oxygen consumption decreases, and electron carriers upstream of Complex IV become more reduced.
ATP synthase pumps protons out of the matrix to replace Complex IV.
Succinate oxidation bypasses the need for oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor.

questions.explanation: Complex IV transfers electrons to oxygen. Blocking it decreases oxygen consumption and causes upstream carriers to remain reduced because they cannot pass electrons efficiently to the terminal acceptor.

choiceExplanations.A: NADH would tend to accumulate in the reduced state when electron flow is blocked.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly predicts both oxygen use and upstream redox state.
choiceExplanations.C: ATP synthase uses the proton gradient; it does not replace Complex IV as a proton pump in the electron transport chain.
choiceExplanations.D: Succinate donates electrons through Complex II, but oxygen is still the terminal electron acceptor downstream.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking entry at Complex II bypasses all downstream respiratory requirements.

questions.stem: Which gluconeogenic enzyme bypasses the irreversible phosphofructokinase-1 step of glycolysis?

choices.text: Hexokinase
Fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase
Pyruvate kinase
Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase

questions.explanation: Fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase converts fructose 1,6-bisphosphate to fructose 6-phosphate, bypassing the PFK-1 reaction in gluconeogenesis. This step is reciprocally regulated with glycolysis.

choiceExplanations.A: Hexokinase catalyzes the first irreversible glycolytic step and is bypassed by glucose 6-phosphatase in gluconeogenesis.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the gluconeogenic bypass for the PFK-1 step.
choiceExplanations.C: Pyruvate kinase is bypassed by pyruvate carboxylase and phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase.
choiceExplanations.D: Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase catalyzes a reversible shared step, not an irreversible bypass.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing the glycolytic enzyme rather than the gluconeogenic bypass enzyme.

questions.stem: Which observation best supports the idea that RNA can have catalytic function as well as information-storage function?

choices.text: Some RNA molecules participate directly in splicing and peptide bond formation.
DNA contains thymine instead of uracil.
Proteins are polymers of amino acids joined by peptide bonds.
Lipids can form bilayers spontaneously in water.

questions.explanation: Ribozymes are catalytic RNAs. Examples include RNA components of the spliceosome and the ribosomal peptidyl transferase center, supporting the dual informational and catalytic potential of RNA.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies catalytic roles for RNA.
choiceExplanations.B: The presence of thymine in DNA distinguishes DNA from RNA but does not show RNA catalysis.
choiceExplanations.C: Protein polymer chemistry does not demonstrate catalytic RNA.
choiceExplanations.D: Bilayer formation is important for membranes but unrelated to RNA catalysis.

questions.commonMistake: Associating catalysis exclusively with proteins.

questions.stem: Assuming A and B assort independently, what F2 phenotypic ratio is expected from selfing AaBb progeny in this pathway?

choices.text: 9 purple : 4 blue : 3 white
9 purple : 3 blue : 4 white
1 purple : 2 blue : 1 white
3 purple : 1 white only

questions.explanation: Purple requires at least one functional A allele and one functional B allele (A_B_), giving 9/16. Blue requires A_bb, giving 3/16. White occurs when aa prevents intermediate formation regardless of B genotype, giving 4/16.

choiceExplanations.A: This swaps the blue and white classes.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies recessive epistasis of aa over the B locus.
choiceExplanations.C: This is a one-gene heterozygote ratio, not a two-gene F2 pathway ratio.
choiceExplanations.D: This ignores the distinct blue phenotype produced by A_bb genotypes.

questions.commonMistake: Using a standard 9:3:3:1 ratio without combining the aaB_ and aabb white classes.

questions.stem: In the pigment pathway, which genetic interaction best describes the effect of aa on colony color?

choices.text: X-linked inheritance, because both genes are autosomal
Codominance, because A and B are both expressed equally in heterozygotes
Genomic imprinting, because phenotype depends on the parent of origin
Recessive epistasis, because aa masks the phenotypic effect of the B locus

questions.explanation: The passage states that loss of enzyme A produces white colonies regardless of enzyme B genotype. Thus the homozygous recessive aa genotype masks B-locus variation, which is recessive epistasis.

choiceExplanations.A: The passage states the genes are autosomal, not X-linked.
choiceExplanations.B: Codominance involves simultaneous expression of different alleles at one locus, not masking across loci.
choiceExplanations.C: No parent-of-origin effect is described.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly describes aa masking the B locus.

questions.commonMistake: Calling any two-gene interaction incomplete dominance rather than identifying masking across loci.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the biochemical behavior of the b Gly45Asp enzyme B variant?

choices.text: The substitution changes the colorless precursor directly into purple pigment without enzyme A.
The substitution prevents transcription of the B gene, eliminating enzyme B protein.
The substitution greatly increases Km but has no effect on Vmax.
The substitution destabilizes enzyme B dimerization, reducing Vmax while leaving substrate affinity nearly unchanged.

questions.explanation: Table p4_t2 shows low dimer percentage and reduced Vmax for the Gly45Asp variant, with Km nearly unchanged. The osmolyte partially restores dimerization and Vmax, supporting a quaternary-structure stability defect.

choiceExplanations.A: The pathway requires enzyme A to make the blue intermediate before enzyme B acts.
choiceExplanations.B: Purified enzyme B variant is characterized, so the defect is not simply absence of transcription.
choiceExplanations.C: Km changes only from 0.10 to 0.12 mM, whereas Vmax falls strongly.
choiceExplanations.D: This integrates the native-gel and kinetic data correctly.

questions.commonMistake: Looking only at the mutation name and not comparing Km, Vmax, and dimerization data.

questions.stem: What phenotypic ratio is expected from crossing Aabb yeast with aaBb yeast?

choices.text: 3 purple : 1 blue only
9 purple : 3 blue : 4 white
1 purple : 1 white only
1 purple : 1 blue : 2 white

questions.explanation: Aabb can produce Ab or ab gametes. aaBb can produce aB or ab gametes. The offspring are AaBb purple, Aabb blue, aaBb white, and aabb white in equal genotypic proportions, giving 1 purple : 1 blue : 2 white.

choiceExplanations.A: This ignores the white classes caused by aa.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the F2 selfing ratio for AaBb, not this cross.
choiceExplanations.C: This ignores the blue Aabb class.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly combines the two white genotypes caused by aa.

questions.commonMistake: Applying the F2 ratio without constructing the gametes for the specified cross.

questions.stem: The F2 cross produced 320 total colonies. If the expected ratio is 9:3:4, which conclusion is most reasonable from Table p4_t1?

choices.text: The data are close to the expected counts for independent assortment with recessive epistasis.
The data prove that A and B are the same gene.
The blue class is absent, so enzyme B is unnecessary.
The observed white count is too low to be compatible with any epistatic model.

questions.explanation: For 320 colonies, a 9:3:4 ratio predicts 180 purple, 60 blue, and 80 white colonies. The observed counts, 178, 59, and 83, are very close to those expectations.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly compares observed counts to the expected ratio.
choiceExplanations.B: The three phenotypic classes and pathway model do not prove the genes are identical; they support two loci.
choiceExplanations.C: There are 59 blue colonies, and enzyme B is required to convert blue intermediate to purple.
choiceExplanations.D: The white count is close to the expected 80.

questions.commonMistake: Judging a ratio by exact equality rather than by reasonable sampling variation around expected counts.

questions.stem: Two genes are followed in a dihybrid cross. Which condition most directly supports use of the multiplication rule for independent assortment of the two genes?

choices.text: The genes encode proteins in the same biochemical pathway.
The genes are on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome.
The dominant allele at one locus is always lethal.
The two genes have identical DNA sequences.

questions.explanation: Independent assortment applies when allele segregation at one locus is independent of allele segregation at the other, which is expected for genes on different chromosomes or sufficiently far apart on the same chromosome.

choiceExplanations.A: Being in the same pathway does not guarantee independent inheritance.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the chromosomal basis for independent assortment.
choiceExplanations.C: Dominant lethality changes observed ratios and does not support independent assortment calculations.
choiceExplanations.D: Identical DNA sequences would not define two independently assorting loci.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing biochemical interaction between gene products with independent chromosomal assortment.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes how mitochondrial ATP synthase uses the proton gradient?

choices.text: ATP synthase pumps electrons from NADH to oxygen while making ATP.
Protons directly donate phosphate groups to ADP in the matrix.
Proton flow through the Fo portion drives rotation that changes F1 conformations to promote ATP formation.
The F1 portion hydrolyzes oxygen to create the proton gradient.

questions.explanation: Chemiosmotic coupling uses proton flow down the electrochemical gradient through Fo to drive mechanical rotation and conformational changes in F1 that favor ATP synthesis from ADP and phosphate.

choiceExplanations.A: Electron transfer to oxygen occurs in the electron transport chain, especially Complex IV, not ATP synthase.
choiceExplanations.B: Protons do not donate phosphate groups; inorganic phosphate is the phosphate source.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly describes the rotary mechanism of ATP synthase.
choiceExplanations.D: Oxygen is reduced by Complex IV; F1 does not hydrolyze oxygen to form the gradient.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking the proton gradient contributes chemical phosphate rather than mechanical driving force.

questions.stem: A reducing agent breaks disulfide bonds in a secreted protein but does not hydrolyze peptide bonds. Which level of structure is directly disrupted while primary structure is preserved?

choices.text: Tertiary or quaternary structure stabilized by covalent cross-links
The amino acid sequence from N-terminus to C-terminus
The genetic code used by ribosomes
The phosphodiester backbone of the corresponding mRNA

questions.explanation: Disulfide bonds are covalent cross-links between cysteine side chains that can stabilize tertiary or quaternary structure. Breaking them can unfold or dissociate a protein without changing the peptide-bonded primary sequence.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies the structural role of disulfide bonds.
choiceExplanations.B: Primary structure would change only if peptide bonds or residue identities changed.
choiceExplanations.C: A reducing agent acting on protein disulfides does not alter the genetic code.
choiceExplanations.D: mRNA phosphodiester bonds are not protein disulfide bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Treating all covalent bonds in proteins as part of primary structure.

questions.stem: The oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway is most directly responsible for producing which products from glucose 6-phosphate?

choices.text: NADPH, CO2, and ribulose 5-phosphate
NADH, pyruvate, and ATP
FADH2, acetyl-CoA, and oxaloacetate
GTP, lactate, and glycogen

questions.explanation: The oxidative PPP converts glucose 6-phosphate to ribulose 5-phosphate while producing NADPH and releasing CO2. NADPH supports reductive biosynthesis and antioxidant defense.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies the main outputs of the oxidative PPP.
choiceExplanations.B: These are associated more closely with glycolysis and redox metabolism, not the oxidative PPP as stated.
choiceExplanations.C: These belong to mitochondrial metabolism, not the oxidative PPP.
choiceExplanations.D: These are not the direct products of the oxidative PPP.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing NADPH-producing pathways with NADH-producing glycolytic or mitochondrial pathways.

questions.stem: A newly translated protein is destined for secretion from a eukaryotic cell. Which event most directly targets the ribosome-nascent chain complex to the rough endoplasmic reticulum?

choices.text: Recognition of an N-terminal signal peptide by the signal recognition particle
Addition of a 5' cap to the protein's N-terminus
Base pairing between the protein and a DNA promoter
Release of cytochrome c into the cytosol

questions.explanation: Secretory proteins commonly contain an N-terminal signal peptide that is recognized by signal recognition particle, pausing translation and targeting the complex to the rough ER for cotranslational translocation.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the canonical targeting mechanism for many secreted proteins.
choiceExplanations.B: A 5' cap is an mRNA modification, not a protein N-terminal modification.
choiceExplanations.C: Proteins do not base pair with DNA promoters as part of ER targeting.
choiceExplanations.D: Cytochrome c release is related to apoptosis, not secretory protein targeting.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing mRNA processing signals with protein targeting signals.

questions.stem: Why is 14CO2 production from [1-14C]glucose much higher than from [6-14C]glucose in wild-type cells?

choices.text: Glycolysis releases carbon 6 of glucose as CO2 during lactate formation.
The oxidative PPP releases carbon 1 of glucose 6-phosphate as CO2.
ATP synthase oxidizes carbon 1 directly to CO2.
Glucose carbon 6 is converted into the phosphate group of ATP.

questions.explanation: The passage states that the oxidative PPP releases carbon 1 of glucose 6-phosphate as CO2 while producing NADPH. Therefore, [1-14C]glucose is a reporter of oxidative PPP flux in these cells.

choiceExplanations.A: Lactate fermentation does not release CO2 from glucose.
choiceExplanations.B: This directly follows from the passage description of the oxidative PPP.
choiceExplanations.C: ATP synthase uses a proton gradient and does not oxidize glucose carbons.
choiceExplanations.D: ATP phosphate groups are not formed from glucose carbon atoms.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming all glucose oxidation labels behave identically regardless of pathway chemistry.

questions.stem: Based on Table p5_t2, which kinetic effect best describes the G6PD-M mutation?

choices.text: A large decrease in catalytic capacity with little change in apparent substrate affinity
A large increase in Km with unchanged Vmax
Competitive inhibition by NADPH at the glucose 6-phosphate binding site
Complete absence of glucose 6-phosphate binding

questions.explanation: G6PD-M has a Vmax only 30% of wild type, while Km changes minimally from 0.08 to 0.09 mM. This indicates reduced catalytic capacity or active enzyme performance rather than a major substrate-binding defect.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly compares the Vmax and Km values.
choiceExplanations.B: Km changes very little, whereas Vmax changes substantially.
choiceExplanations.C: The table compares purified enzymes and does not show NADPH inhibition.
choiceExplanations.D: If glucose 6-phosphate binding were absent, a meaningful Km near wild type would not be expected.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting any loss of pathway flux as an increased Km without checking the kinetic parameters.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the wild-type response to oxidant exposure in Table p5_t1?

choices.text: Wild-type cells cannot use glucose 6-phosphate in more than one pathway.
Oxidant exposure completely shuts down glycolysis in wild-type cells.
Oxidant exposure causes carbon 6 of glucose to be released preferentially as CO2.
Oxidant exposure increases oxidative PPP flux to support NADPH-dependent redox defense.

questions.explanation: In wild-type cells, oxidant exposure increases 14CO2 from [1-14C]glucose from 100% to 180%, consistent with increased oxidative PPP flux. The NADPH/NADP+ and GSH/GSSG ratios fall but remain higher than in oxidant-treated G6PD-M cells, supporting a redox-defense role.

choiceExplanations.A: The figure shows glucose 6-phosphate can enter either glycolysis or the PPP.
choiceExplanations.B: Lactate release increases to 120%, so glycolysis is not completely shut down.
choiceExplanations.C: 14CO2 from [6-14C]glucose remains low compared with [1-14C]glucose.
choiceExplanations.D: This integrates the label data with NADPH and glutathione measurements.

questions.commonMistake: Looking only at the decrease in NADPH/NADP+ and missing the increased pathway flux marker.

questions.stem: The differentiated erythroid cells show very low 14CO2 production from [6-14C]glucose. Which passage detail best explains this result?

choices.text: G6PD directly converts carbon 6 into lactate and CO2.
Carbon 6 is the carbon specifically released by the oxidative PPP.
These cells lack mitochondria after differentiation, limiting complete oxidation of glucose carbons through the TCA cycle.
NADPH is made only by oxidative phosphorylation in these cells.

questions.explanation: The passage states that the cells lack mitochondria after differentiation and that the oxidative PPP releases carbon 1, not carbon 6, as CO2. Without mitochondrial oxidation through the TCA cycle, carbon 6 is not efficiently released as CO2.

choiceExplanations.A: G6PD acts on glucose 6-phosphate in the oxidative PPP and does not directly convert carbon 6 to lactate and CO2.
choiceExplanations.B: The passage states carbon 1 is released in the oxidative PPP.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly uses both the mitochondrial limitation and the carbon specificity of the PPP.
choiceExplanations.D: NADPH in the passage is generated by the oxidative PPP, not oxidative phosphorylation.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming low carbon-6 CO2 means the radiolabel was not taken up rather than using the pathway map.

questions.stem: Which statement best explains the low viability of oxidant-treated G6PD-M cells?

choices.text: Reduced G6PD Vmax limits NADPH production, impairing glutathione reduction during oxidative stress.
G6PD-M cells cannot perform lactate fermentation, so NAD+ is not regenerated.
The mutation increases oxidative phosphorylation until reactive oxygen species disappear.
The oxidative PPP directly synthesizes ATP, so lower G6PD activity eliminates all ATP production.

questions.explanation: G6PD-M has low Vmax and low [1-14C]CO2 production, indicating impaired oxidative PPP flux. Under oxidant stress, these cells have very low NADPH/NADP+ and GSH/GSSG ratios, consistent with inadequate NADPH-dependent glutathione reduction and reduced survival.

choiceExplanations.A: This best connects enzyme kinetics, PPP flux, redox measurements, and viability.
choiceExplanations.B: Lactate release is high in G6PD-M cells, indicating glycolytic fermentation continues.
choiceExplanations.C: The passage states these differentiated cells lack mitochondria, and the data do not show increased oxidative phosphorylation.
choiceExplanations.D: The oxidative PPP produces NADPH, not ATP directly; these cells rely on glycolysis for ATP.

questions.commonMistake: Conflating NADPH production with ATP production and overlooking lactate data.

questions.stem: A protein has an isoelectric point of 6.2. It is loaded at pH 8.0 onto an anion-exchange resin that binds negatively charged proteins. Which outcome is most likely?

choices.text: The protein will precipitate because pH 8.0 is below its pI.
The protein will bind the resin because it has a net positive charge.
The protein will pass through because proteins have no charge above their pI.
The protein will bind the resin because it has a net negative charge.

questions.explanation: At a pH above its isoelectric point, a protein has a net negative charge. An anion-exchange resin binds anions, so the protein is expected to bind and can later be eluted by increasing salt or changing pH.

choiceExplanations.A: pH 8.0 is above, not below, a pI of 6.2.
choiceExplanations.B: A protein is generally net positive below its pI, not above it.
choiceExplanations.C: Proteins have zero net charge at their pI, not at all pH values above the pI.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly relates pH above pI to net negative charge and anion-exchange binding.

questions.commonMistake: Reversing the sign of a protein's net charge above versus below its pI.

questions.stem: An inhibitor lowers both the apparent Km and apparent Vmax of a Michaelis-Menten enzyme by the same factor. Which mechanism best explains this kinetic pattern?

choices.text: The inhibitor binds only the enzyme-substrate complex.
The inhibitor competes with substrate for the free enzyme active site.
The inhibitor permanently destroys the enzyme's primary structure.
The inhibitor increases substrate diffusion to the active site.

questions.explanation: Uncompetitive inhibitors bind only the enzyme-substrate complex. In the simple Michaelis-Menten model, this decreases both apparent Km and apparent Vmax by the same factor.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the defining binding pattern and kinetic signature of uncompetitive inhibition.
choiceExplanations.B: Competitive inhibition increases apparent Km while leaving Vmax unchanged.
choiceExplanations.C: Irreversible destruction would mainly decrease Vmax and would not predict the matched decrease in apparent Km.
choiceExplanations.D: Improved substrate diffusion would not explain a decreased Vmax.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming every reversible inhibitor must bind the free enzyme active site.

questions.stem: A DNA template strand contains the sequence 3'-TAC GGA CTT ACT-5'. What mRNA sequence would be produced from this template region?

choices.text: 5'-UAC GGA CUU ACU-3'
5'-TAC GGA CTT ACT-3'
3'-AUG CCU GAA UGA-5'
5'-AUG CCU GAA UGA-3'

questions.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand antiparallel and synthesizes RNA 5' to 3'. The complement of 3'-TAC GGA CTT ACT-5' is 5'-AUG CCU GAA UGA-3'.

choiceExplanations.A: This is not the correct complement of the provided template.
choiceExplanations.B: This repeats the DNA template and uses thymine rather than uracil.
choiceExplanations.C: This has the correct base complement but is written in the wrong orientation for the mRNA product.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the correct antiparallel RNA complement with uracil replacing thymine.

questions.commonMistake: Complementing the bases but writing the product strand in the wrong direction.

questions.stem: A rapidly dividing cell has abundant NADPH but needs more ribose 5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis. Which metabolic strategy best matches this need?

choices.text: Convert ribose 5-phosphate directly into lactate to regenerate NAD+.
Run the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway repeatedly to maximize CO2 release from glucose carbon 1.
Block all transketolase reactions so that ribose 5-phosphate cannot equilibrate with glycolytic intermediates.
Use nonoxidative pentose phosphate pathway reactions to convert glycolytic intermediates into ribose 5-phosphate.

questions.explanation: When a cell needs ribose 5-phosphate more than NADPH, the reversible nonoxidative reactions of the pentose phosphate pathway can convert fructose 6-phosphate and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate into ribose 5-phosphate without requiring additional oxidative NADPH production.

choiceExplanations.A: Ribose 5-phosphate is used for nucleotide synthesis, not direct lactate fermentation.
choiceExplanations.B: The oxidative phase is useful for NADPH generation and CO2 release, which is not the stated need.
choiceExplanations.C: Blocking transketolase would impair interconversion between pentose phosphates and glycolytic intermediates.
choiceExplanations.D: This directly supplies ribose 5-phosphate from glycolytic intermediates while avoiding excess NADPH production.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming ribose 5-phosphate production must always be coupled to new NADPH production.

questions.stem: Malonate is a structural analog of succinate that inhibits succinate dehydrogenase. Which immediate effect is most likely in isolated mitochondria supplied primarily with succinate?

choices.text: Increased conversion of oxygen to water by Complex IV
Increased proton pumping by Complex II
Direct inhibition of ATP synthase by preventing proton flow through Fo
Reduced electron entry through Complex II and decreased ubiquinone reduction

questions.explanation: Succinate dehydrogenase is Complex II. Inhibiting it reduces electron transfer from succinate-derived FADH2 to ubiquinone, decreasing downstream electron flow from this entry point.

choiceExplanations.A: Reduced electron entry through Complex II would tend to decrease, not increase, downstream oxygen reduction.
choiceExplanations.B: Complex II does not pump protons, and inhibition would not increase its activity.
choiceExplanations.C: Malonate targets succinate dehydrogenase, not ATP synthase.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly identifies succinate dehydrogenase as Complex II and predicts less ubiquinone reduction.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting that Complex II transfers electrons to ubiquinone but does not pump protons.

questions.stem: Which mechanism best describes the effect of PYA on wild-type LDH-R?

choices.text: Competitive inhibition with respect to pyruvate
Uncompetitive inhibition with respect to pyruvate
Irreversible tetramer dissociation
Allosteric activation that increases Vmax

questions.explanation: PYA increases the apparent Km for pyruvate from 0.10 mM to 0.55 mM while leaving Vmax nearly unchanged. Because PYA is a pyruvate analog, this pattern is most consistent with competition at or near the pyruvate-binding site.

choiceExplanations.A: This matches increased Km with essentially unchanged Vmax.
choiceExplanations.B: Uncompetitive inhibition would lower both apparent Km and Vmax.
choiceExplanations.C: Tetrameric protein remains 93%, arguing against major tetramer dissociation.
choiceExplanations.D: Vmax does not increase in the presence of PYA.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying an active-site analog as allosteric without using the Km and Vmax pattern.

questions.stem: The H177Q variant has nearly normal pyruvate and NADH Km values but very low Vmax. Which interpretation is most directly supported?

choices.text: His177 forms the entire NADH-binding pocket.
His177 is required for LDH-R tetramer assembly.
His177 prevents pyruvate from binding to the active site.
His177 contributes to catalytic chemistry after substrate binding.

questions.explanation: H177Q remains mostly tetrameric and retains near-normal Km values for pyruvate and NADH, but its Vmax falls to 6%. This supports a defect in catalytic turnover, such as acid-base chemistry or transition-state stabilization, rather than a major substrate-binding or assembly defect.

choiceExplanations.A: The NADH Km is nearly unchanged, so His177 is unlikely to form the entire NADH-binding pocket.
choiceExplanations.B: Tetrameric protein is 92%, which argues against a tetramer assembly requirement.
choiceExplanations.C: The pyruvate Km is nearly unchanged, so pyruvate binding is not the main defect.
choiceExplanations.D: This best fits preserved binding and assembly with severely impaired catalysis.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that low activity must reflect poor substrate binding rather than impaired catalytic turnover.

questions.stem: What is the most likely explanation for the reduced Vmax of the L246D variant?

choices.text: The substitution makes NADH unable to bind LDH-R.
The substitution eliminates pyruvate binding at the active site.
The substitution disrupts quaternary structure needed for maximal catalytic output.
The substitution converts LDH-R into an RNA enzyme.

questions.explanation: L246D has a strongly reduced tetrameric fraction and reduced Vmax, while pyruvate and NADH Km values are close to wild type. This supports a quaternary-structure defect that reduces maximal catalytic performance.

choiceExplanations.A: The NADH Km remains close to wild type, so NADH binding is not eliminated.
choiceExplanations.B: The pyruvate Km remains close to wild type, so pyruvate binding is not eliminated.
choiceExplanations.C: This integrates the low tetramer percentage with reduced Vmax and nearly normal Km values.
choiceExplanations.D: An amino acid substitution in a protein cannot convert the protein into a ribozyme.

questions.commonMistake: Ignoring oligomeric-state data and attributing every Vmax change to active-site binding.

questions.stem: At saturating NADH and 0.08 mM pyruvate, which condition should give the highest initial velocity among the choices below?

choices.text: C-terminal tail deletion, pH 7.4
Wild type + PYA, pH 7.4
Wild type, pH 6.6

questions.explanation: At a low pyruvate concentration, conditions with lower Km and higher Vmax are favored. Wild type at pH 6.6 has the lowest Km listed and the highest Vmax, so it should have the greatest initial velocity among the options.

choiceExplanations.A: The tail deletion has lower Vmax and a higher pyruvate Km than wild type at pH 6.6.
choiceExplanations.B: PYA increases the pyruvate Km substantially, so low pyruvate would poorly support velocity.
choiceExplanations.C: H177Q has only 6% relative Vmax.
choiceExplanations.D: This condition combines high Vmax with strong apparent affinity for pyruvate.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing an enzyme condition by Vmax alone or Km alone rather than considering both at the stated substrate concentration.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best test whether the C-terminal tail deletion directly weakens NADH binding rather than globally unfolding LDH-R?

choices.text: Boil both proteins in SDS and compare only their apparent molecular weights.
Measure lactate concentration in whole cells overexpressing unrelated glycolytic enzymes.
Measure NADH binding to purified wild-type and tail-deleted LDH-R while also assessing folding by circular dichroism or thermal stability.
Sequence the mRNA encoding LDH-R but do not measure protein behavior.

questions.explanation: A direct NADH-binding assay can determine whether the deletion weakens cofactor binding, while an independent folding assay addresses the alternative explanation of global misfolding. The design targets both competing hypotheses.

choiceExplanations.A: Denaturing SDS-PAGE gives subunit size, not native folding or NADH-binding affinity.
choiceExplanations.B: Whole-cell lactate is indirect and confounded by many pathways.
choiceExplanations.C: This directly tests cofactor binding and includes a control for overall protein folding.
