choiceExplanations.D: mRNA sequence does not directly test the biochemical effect of the protein deletion.

questions.commonMistake: Selecting an indirect cellular readout when the competing explanations involve purified protein binding and folding.

questions.stem: A buried Asp-Lys salt bridge stabilizes a protein's tertiary structure. Replacing the lysine with leucine is most likely to have which effect?

choices.text: Destabilization caused by loss of a favorable ionic interaction
Stabilization caused by creation of a new disulfide bond
No effect because lysine and leucine are both positively charged
Conversion of the protein backbone into a nucleic acid backbone

questions.explanation: Lysine is positively charged at physiological pH and can form a salt bridge with negatively charged aspartate. Leucine is nonpolar and cannot maintain that ionic interaction, so the tertiary structure may be destabilized.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies the lost salt bridge as the main predicted effect.
choiceExplanations.B: Leucine does not form disulfide bonds; cysteine residues do.
choiceExplanations.C: Leucine is nonpolar, not positively charged.
choiceExplanations.D: An amino acid substitution does not alter the chemical identity of the protein backbone.

questions.commonMistake: Classifying leucine as basic because it is sometimes grouped with hydrophobic residues in protein interiors.

questions.stem: Two double-stranded DNA fragments have the same length. Fragment 1 is 70% GC, whereas Fragment 2 is 30% GC. Which comparison is most likely?

choices.text: Fragment 1 cannot form a double helix because GC pairs are not complementary.
Fragment 2 has a higher melting temperature because AT pairs contain more hydrogen bonds.
Both fragments must have identical melting temperatures because length alone determines DNA stability.
Fragment 1 has a higher melting temperature because GC-rich DNA is generally more thermally stable.

questions.explanation: GC-rich DNA typically melts at a higher temperature because GC base pairs have three hydrogen bonds and favorable stacking interactions relative to AT-rich DNA of the same length.

choiceExplanations.A: G and C are complementary Watson-Crick partners.
choiceExplanations.B: AT pairs have two hydrogen bonds, not more than GC pairs.
choiceExplanations.C: Length matters, but base composition also affects melting temperature.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly predicts higher thermal stability for the GC-rich fragment.

questions.commonMistake: Overlooking base composition and treating all same-length DNA duplexes as equally stable.

questions.stem: An enzyme has a Hill coefficient of 0.6 for its substrate. Which interpretation is most consistent with this value?

choices.text: Binding of substrate to one site increases substrate affinity at other sites.
Binding of substrate to one site decreases substrate affinity at other sites.
The enzyme is inactive because Hill coefficients below 1 are impossible.
The enzyme has exactly 0.6 active sites per molecule.

questions.explanation: A Hill coefficient less than 1 indicates negative cooperativity, meaning substrate binding at one site lowers the apparent affinity of other binding sites. It does not directly equal the number of active sites.

choiceExplanations.A: This describes positive cooperativity, which corresponds to a Hill coefficient greater than 1.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly describes negative cooperativity.
choiceExplanations.C: Hill coefficients below 1 can occur and are interpreted as negative cooperativity.
choiceExplanations.D: The Hill coefficient is not simply the number of active sites.

questions.commonMistake: Treating the Hill coefficient as a literal count of active sites.

questions.stem: In a model plant, loss of enzyme Q is caused by a completely recessive allele q. Two heterozygous plants are crossed. Among offspring that have normal enzyme Q activity, what fraction are expected to be carriers?
questions.explanation: A Qq x Qq cross produces genotypes 1 QQ : 2 Qq : 1 qq. Among offspring with normal enzyme activity, qq is excluded, leaving 1 QQ and 2 Qq. Therefore, 2 of the 3 normal-activity offspring are carriers.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the fraction of all offspring expected to be qq, not the carrier fraction among normal offspring.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the fraction of normal offspring expected to be QQ.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the carrier fraction among all offspring, not conditional on normal activity.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly applies conditional probability after excluding recessive-loss offspring.

questions.commonMistake: Using the carrier fraction among all offspring rather than conditioning on normal phenotype.

questions.stem: Oxidation of FADH2 generally yields less ATP than oxidation of NADH in mitochondria. Which explanation best accounts for this difference?

choices.text: FADH2 oxidation consumes the proton gradient rather than contributing to it.
FADH2 donates electrons directly to ATP synthase.
NADH is oxidized only in the cytosol and never donates electrons to mitochondria.
FADH2-derived electrons enter at Complex II and bypass proton pumping at Complex I.

questions.explanation: NADH donates electrons through Complex I, which pumps protons. FADH2-derived electrons often enter through Complex II, which does not pump protons, so fewer protons are moved per electron pair and less ATP is generated.

choiceExplanations.A: FADH2 oxidation supports electron transport; it does not directly consume the proton gradient.
choiceExplanations.B: ATP synthase uses proton flow and does not directly accept electrons from FADH2.
choiceExplanations.C: Mitochondrial NADH donates electrons to Complex I.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly identifies the bypass of Complex I proton pumping.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking ATP yield depends only on electron number rather than the entry point into the electron transport chain.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the RBP-K knockdown data in Table p7_t1?

choices.text: RBP-K directly converts LUM1 protein monomers into DNA.
RBP-K acts as the catalytic subunit of DNA polymerase.
RBP-K promotes exon 3 inclusion without substantially increasing LUM1 transcription initiation.
RBP-K knockdown increases exon 3 inclusion but blocks translation elongation.

questions.explanation: RBP-K knockdown leaves the pre-mRNA transcription rate essentially unchanged but sharply reduces the fraction of mature mRNA containing exon 3. This indicates a role in splicing regulation rather than transcription initiation.

choiceExplanations.A: Proteins are not converted into DNA by RNA-binding proteins.
choiceExplanations.B: The passage describes an RNA-binding splicing regulator, not DNA polymerase.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly compares unchanged transcription with reduced exon 3 inclusion.
choiceExplanations.D: Exon 3 inclusion decreases, not increases, after RBP-K knockdown.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting lower protein abundance as evidence of lower transcription without checking pre-mRNA transcription rate.

questions.stem: The intron 2 splice donor mutant has a normal pre-mRNA transcription rate but greatly reduced total mature mRNA. Which mechanism best explains this pattern?

choices.text: The mutation changes every exon into an intron before transcription begins.
The mutation prevents RNA polymerase from binding the DNA template.
The mutation increases translation initiation at the Kozak sequence.
Intron retention introduces a premature termination codon that promotes mRNA degradation.

questions.explanation: The passage states that the intron 2 donor mutation causes intron retention and creates a premature termination codon predicted to trigger nonsense-mediated mRNA decay. This explains normal transcription but low mature mRNA.

choiceExplanations.A: Exon and intron identity is not globally converted before transcription.
choiceExplanations.B: Pre-mRNA transcription rate remains 100%, so polymerase recruitment is not the main defect.
choiceExplanations.C: Protein is very low, and the mutation is in a splice donor, not the Kozak sequence.
choiceExplanations.D: This directly fits the passage model and the mRNA data.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming low mature mRNA must be caused by low transcription rather than post-transcriptional decay.

questions.stem: Which process is most directly impaired in the Kozak sequence mutant?

choices.text: Protein dimerization by the exon 3 helix
Spliceosome recognition of exon 3
DNA base pairing during replication
Eukaryotic translation initiation

questions.explanation: The Kozak sequence helps eukaryotic ribosomes identify the start codon. The mutant has nearly normal mature mRNA and exon 3 inclusion but much lower protein, which is consistent with impaired translation initiation.

choiceExplanations.A: Among detected protein, dimeric LUM1 remains high, so dimerization is not the primary defect.
choiceExplanations.B: Exon 3 inclusion remains high in the Kozak mutant.
choiceExplanations.C: The mutation affects mRNA translation, not DNA replication.
choiceExplanations.D: This matches the role of the Kozak sequence and the observed low protein with preserved mRNA.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing start-codon context effects with mRNA splicing defects.

questions.stem: What best explains the effect of the exon 3 hydrophobic-to-charged coding mutant?

choices.text: It increases the percentage of LUM1 protein that is dimeric.
It prevents exon 3 from being transcribed into pre-mRNA.
It causes the spliceosome to remove all mature mRNA caps.
It disrupts the protein-protein interface required for LUM1 dimerization.

questions.explanation: The coding mutant leaves transcription, exon 3 inclusion, and total mature mRNA nearly normal but greatly reduces dimeric LUM1. Replacing hydrophobic residues in a dimerization helix with charged residues is likely to disrupt the protein interface.

choiceExplanations.A: Dimeric LUM1 decreases from 80% to 20%.
choiceExplanations.B: Pre-mRNA transcription and exon 3-containing mRNA remain high.
choiceExplanations.C: The spliceosome does not remove mature mRNA caps, and the data do not support cap loss.
choiceExplanations.D: This directly connects the coding change to reduced dimer formation.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing a coding-region protein-structure defect to altered splicing despite normal exon inclusion.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best test whether the low mature mRNA in the intron 2 splice donor mutant is caused by nonsense-mediated decay?

choices.text: Replace exon 3 with a mitochondrial electron transport protein and measure oxygen consumption only.
Increase the amount of charged tRNA in the culture medium and measure DNA replication.
Reduce a core nonsense-mediated decay factor and measure whether the intron-retaining LUM1 transcript accumulates.
Boil the cells before RNA extraction to improve splice-site recognition.

questions.explanation: If nonsense-mediated decay is degrading the mutant transcript, reducing an essential decay factor should increase the abundance of the intron-retaining mRNA. Measuring the specific transcript tests the proposed mechanism directly.

choiceExplanations.A: Oxygen consumption would not isolate nonsense-mediated decay of LUM1 mRNA.
choiceExplanations.B: Charged tRNAs do not directly test mRNA decay of a premature-stop transcript.
choiceExplanations.C: This experiment directly perturbs the proposed decay pathway and measures the relevant RNA species.
choiceExplanations.D: Boiling cells would damage biomolecules and would not improve splice-site recognition.

questions.commonMistake: Testing protein output instead of directly testing the RNA decay mechanism proposed by the passage.

questions.stem: During oxidation of lactate to pyruvate, NAD+ is converted to NADH. Which statement best describes the role of NAD+ in this reaction?

choices.text: NAD+ forms a peptide bond with pyruvate.
NAD+ is oxidized by donating electrons to lactate.
NAD+ is reduced by accepting electrons as a hydride equivalent.
NAD+ is converted into ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation.

questions.explanation: When lactate is oxidized to pyruvate, NAD+ accepts reducing equivalents and becomes NADH. Gain of electrons is reduction.

choiceExplanations.A: NAD+ does not form peptide bonds with pyruvate.
choiceExplanations.B: NAD+ is reduced, not oxidized, in this reaction.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly describes NAD+ as the electron acceptor that is reduced to NADH.
choiceExplanations.D: NADH formation is a redox event, not substrate-level phosphorylation.

questions.commonMistake: Mixing up oxidation and reduction from the perspective of the cofactor.

questions.stem: Which molecule is a nucleotide rather than a nucleoside?

choices.text: Guanosine
Adenosine
Adenosine monophosphate
Cytidine

questions.explanation: A nucleotide contains a nitrogenous base, a sugar, and at least one phosphate group. Adenosine monophosphate contains adenine, ribose, and phosphate, whereas adenosine, guanosine, and cytidine are nucleosides without phosphate in their names.

choiceExplanations.A: Guanosine is a nucleoside composed of guanine plus ribose.
choiceExplanations.B: Adenosine is a nucleoside composed of adenine plus ribose.
choiceExplanations.C: This contains a phosphate group and is therefore a nucleotide.
choiceExplanations.D: Cytidine is a nucleoside composed of cytosine plus ribose.

questions.commonMistake: Using the base identity rather than the presence of phosphate to distinguish nucleotides from nucleosides.

questions.stem: A covalent inhibitor modifies an essential active-site residue in 30% of enzyme molecules. After excess inhibitor is removed by dialysis, activity remains 70% of the original value. Which kinetic effect is most likely when substrate is varied?

choices.text: The enzyme becomes positively cooperative even if it was monomeric.
Km increases, while Vmax remains unchanged.
Both Km and Vmax increase because dialysis activates the enzyme.
Vmax decreases, while the Km of the remaining active enzyme is largely unchanged.

questions.explanation: Irreversible inactivation reduces the concentration of active enzyme molecules. This lowers Vmax, which depends on active enzyme concentration, while the substrate affinity of the remaining unmodified enzyme molecules can remain approximately the same.

choiceExplanations.A: Irreversible modification does not by itself create cooperativity in a monomeric enzyme.
choiceExplanations.B: This pattern is typical of competitive inhibition, not irreversible loss of enzyme molecules.
choiceExplanations.C: Dialysis removes free inhibitor but does not reverse covalent modification in the stem.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the expected effect of reducing active enzyme concentration.

questions.commonMistake: Treating irreversible inhibition as though it must change substrate affinity of the remaining active enzyme.

questions.stem: A mutation reduces the activity of phosphoglycerate kinase in glycolysis. Which direct consequence is most likely?

choices.text: Direct oxidation of pyruvate to CO2 in the cytosol
Increased production of ribose 5-phosphate by ATP synthase
Reduced ATP formation from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate
Replacement of NAD+ with FAD as the glycolytic electron acceptor

questions.explanation: Phosphoglycerate kinase catalyzes a substrate-level phosphorylation step in which 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate transfers a phosphoryl group to ADP to form ATP and 3-phosphoglycerate.

choiceExplanations.A: Complete oxidation of pyruvate to CO2 does not occur directly in the cytosol.
choiceExplanations.B: ATP synthase is part of oxidative phosphorylation, not ribose 5-phosphate production.
choiceExplanations.C: This is the direct reaction catalyzed by phosphoglycerate kinase.
choiceExplanations.D: Glycolysis uses NAD+ at the glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase step, not FAD as a replacement.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis with ATP synthesis by oxidative phosphorylation.

questions.stem: A mutation disrupts the TATA box of a eukaryotic protein-coding gene. Which process is most directly affected?

choices.text: Base pairing between codons and anticodons during elongation
Hydrolysis of mischarged tRNA in the ribosomal A site
Recruitment of general transcription factors and RNA polymerase II
Proton pumping by Complex IV

questions.explanation: The TATA box is a promoter element that helps position the transcription initiation machinery for many eukaryotic protein-coding genes. Disrupting it most directly impairs transcription initiation by RNA polymerase II.

choiceExplanations.A: Codon-anticodon pairing occurs during translation, not promoter recognition.
choiceExplanations.B: tRNA editing is a translation fidelity process and is unrelated to a TATA-box mutation.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly identifies the promoter-level effect.
choiceExplanations.D: Complex IV is part of oxidative phosphorylation, not transcription initiation.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing promoter mutations with mutations in the translated coding sequence.

questions.stem: According to the passage, which reaction most directly accounts for ATP-to-AMP conversion by ThrRS-F?

choices.text: Peptide bond formation between two amino acids
Formation of an aminoacyl-AMP intermediate
Hydrolysis of the phosphodiester backbone of tRNA
Oxidation of NADH by Complex I

questions.explanation: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases activate amino acids by using ATP to form aminoacyl-AMP intermediates. The passage states that ThrRS-F forms threonyl-AMP from threonine and ATP.

choiceExplanations.A: Peptide bond formation occurs on the ribosome and does not explain ATP-to-AMP conversion by ThrRS-F.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the ATP-dependent activation step described in the passage.
choiceExplanations.C: tRNA backbone hydrolysis is not part of normal aminoacylation.
choiceExplanations.D: Complex I is part of oxidative phosphorylation and is unrelated to ThrRS-F amino acid activation.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing ATP use in tRNA charging to the ribosome rather than the aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the phenotype of the editing-site D201A variant?

choices.text: The variant cannot recognize any tRNA molecule.
Threonine cannot be activated, so ATP-to-AMP conversion is nearly absent.
Amino acid activation is mostly intact, but hydrolysis of mischarged Val-tRNAThr is impaired.
The variant improves reporter activity by eliminating translation errors.

questions.explanation: D201A maintains high Thr-tRNAThr charging and high ATP-to-AMP conversion but accumulates much more Val-tRNAThr and lowers reporter activity. This indicates a specific editing defect rather than failure of activation or tRNA recognition.

choiceExplanations.A: The enzyme still charges wild-type tRNAThr efficiently.
choiceExplanations.B: Charging and ATP-to-AMP conversion remain high, so activation is not nearly absent.
choiceExplanations.C: This matches high charging with increased mischarged tRNA accumulation.
choiceExplanations.D: Reporter activity decreases, consistent with more translation errors.

questions.commonMistake: Treating editing failure as the same as failure to aminoacylate the tRNA.

questions.stem: What is the most likely direct effect of the activation-site K55A substitution?

choices.text: Reduced formation of aminoacyl-AMP intermediates
Enhanced hydrolysis of correctly charged Thr-tRNAThr
Increased recognition of tRNAAla as tRNAThr
Creation of a new stop codon in the reporter mRNA

questions.explanation: K55A has low Thr-tRNAThr charging and low ATP-to-AMP conversion. Because K55 is in the activation site, the best interpretation is that amino acid activation through aminoacyl-AMP formation is impaired.

choiceExplanations.A: This directly explains reduced ATP-to-AMP conversion and poor charging.
choiceExplanations.B: The data do not show excessive hydrolysis of correctly charged tRNA.
choiceExplanations.C: The condition with noncognate tRNAAla is not the K55A condition, and no enhanced recognition is shown.
choiceExplanations.D: The enzyme substitution does not alter the reporter mRNA codons.

questions.commonMistake: Attributing low charging to editing rather than to the ATP-dependent activation step.

questions.stem: The acceptor-stem mutant tRNAThr produces low charging even with wild-type ThrRS-F. Which conclusion is best supported?

choices.text: The acceptor stem is the site where ribosomes synthesize DNA.
The acceptor stem contains identity elements important for recognition by ThrRS-F.
The acceptor-stem mutation increases editing of valine to threonine.
ThrRS-F no longer requires ATP when the acceptor stem is changed.

questions.explanation: Wild-type ThrRS-F charges acceptor-stem mutant tRNAThr poorly, indicating that tRNA structural features in the acceptor stem contribute to proper recognition and aminoacylation.

choiceExplanations.A: Ribosomes synthesize proteins, not DNA.
choiceExplanations.B: This directly follows from poor charging of the altered tRNA.
choiceExplanations.C: Editing hydrolyzes mischarged tRNA; it does not convert valine into threonine.
choiceExplanations.D: ATP-to-AMP conversion is reduced, not made unnecessary.

questions.commonMistake: Viewing tRNAs as interchangeable adapters rather than molecules with recognition determinants.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best test whether the reduced reporter activity in the D201A condition is caused by valine misincorporation at threonine-coded positions?

choices.text: Analyze reporter peptides by mass spectrometry to detect valine at positions normally occupied by threonine.
Measure only total ATP concentration in the culture medium.
Delete all threonine codons from the genetic code.
Assay Complex IV activity in isolated mitochondria.

questions.explanation: Mass spectrometry of reporter peptides can directly identify amino acids incorporated at specific positions. Detecting valine at positions encoded by threonine codons would support the proposed translation-fidelity mechanism.

choiceExplanations.A: This directly tests the predicted molecular error in the reporter protein.
choiceExplanations.B: Total ATP concentration would not reveal which amino acids were incorporated.
choiceExplanations.C: Codons cannot be deleted from the genetic code in this experimental context.
choiceExplanations.D: Complex IV activity is unrelated to reporter mistranslation by ThrRS-F.

questions.commonMistake: Using an indirect activity readout when the hypothesis concerns specific amino acid misincorporation.

questions.stem: Which feature most directly explains why peptide bonds are planar and have restricted rotation?

choices.text: Peptide bonds are phosphodiester bonds between amino acids.
Resonance gives the peptide bond partial double-bond character.
Every peptide bond contains a disulfide linkage.
Peptide bonds are held together only by hydrogen bonds.

questions.explanation: The peptide bond has resonance between the carbonyl carbon, carbonyl oxygen, and amide nitrogen. This gives the C-N bond partial double-bond character, making it planar and restricting rotation.

choiceExplanations.A: Phosphodiester bonds are found in nucleic acid backbones, not between amino acids in proteins.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly explains peptide bond planarity.
choiceExplanations.C: Disulfide linkages occur between cysteine side chains, not in every peptide bond.
choiceExplanations.D: Peptide bonds are covalent amide bonds, not merely hydrogen bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing covalent peptide bond structure with noncovalent interactions that stabilize secondary structure.

questions.stem: A single nucleotide is inserted near the beginning of a coding sequence, and the insertion is not a multiple of three nucleotides. Which outcome is most likely?

choices.text: Improved ribosome proofreading of the original mRNA sequence
A silent mutation because insertions never affect codon reading frame
A substitution of exactly one amino acid with no downstream effect
A frameshift that changes downstream codons and may introduce a premature stop codon

questions.explanation: Insertions or deletions that are not multiples of three shift the reading frame. A frameshift near the start of a coding sequence changes many downstream codons and can produce an early stop codon.

choiceExplanations.A: Ribosome proofreading would not restore the original mRNA sequence.
choiceExplanations.B: Insertions can strongly affect the reading frame.
choiceExplanations.C: A single amino acid substitution is more typical of a missense point mutation, not a frameshift insertion.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly describes the likely consequence of a non-triplet insertion.

questions.commonMistake: Treating a one-base insertion like a one-base substitution.

questions.stem: In liver cells, pyruvate kinase is inhibited by high ATP and activated by fructose 1,6-bisphosphate. Which interpretation best explains this regulation?

choices.text: Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate is the direct product of pyruvate kinase.
High ATP indicates that glycolysis must accelerate regardless of substrate availability.
High ATP signals sufficient energy, while fructose 1,6-bisphosphate provides feed-forward activation when glycolytic flux is high.
Pyruvate kinase is a mitochondrial electron transport complex.

questions.explanation: Pyruvate kinase catalyzes a late glycolytic step. ATP inhibition helps slow glycolysis when energy is abundant, whereas fructose 1,6-bisphosphate indicates upstream glycolytic commitment and activates the downstream step.

choiceExplanations.A: Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate is produced upstream of pyruvate kinase, not by pyruvate kinase.
choiceExplanations.B: High ATP usually inhibits energy-producing catabolic pathways.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly connects energy status and feed-forward regulation.
choiceExplanations.D: Pyruvate kinase is a cytosolic glycolytic enzyme, not an electron transport complex.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting ATP as a substrate-like activator for all metabolic enzymes.

questions.stem: During DNA synthesis, a polymerase adds a new nucleotide to which chemical group on the growing strand?

choices.text: The amino terminus of the first nucleotide
The methyl group of thymine
The free 3' hydroxyl group
The peptide bond of the previous nucleotide

questions.explanation: DNA polymerases extend nucleic acid strands by adding incoming nucleotides to the free 3' hydroxyl of the growing strand, forming a phosphodiester bond and synthesizing in the 5' to 3' direction.

choiceExplanations.A: Nucleotides do not have amino termini analogous to proteins.
choiceExplanations.B: The thymine methyl group is not used to extend DNA.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly identifies the reactive group on the growing strand.
choiceExplanations.D: Nucleotides are joined by phosphodiester bonds, not peptide bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Remembering strand directionality but not the chemical group that is extended.

questions.stem: In mitochondria, the intermembrane space is more acidic than the matrix during active respiration. Which movement is directly coupled to ATP synthesis by ATP synthase?

choices.text: Protons moving from the intermembrane space into the matrix
Protons moving from the matrix into the intermembrane space through ATP synthase
Electrons moving through the Fo subunit into the matrix
Glucose moving through Complex IV into the intermembrane space

questions.explanation: The electron transport chain pumps protons from the matrix to the intermembrane space. ATP synthase uses the downhill return of protons into the matrix to drive ATP formation.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the proton flow used by ATP synthase during ATP production.
choiceExplanations.B: This is the direction of proton pumping by the electron transport chain, not ATP-producing flow through ATP synthase.
choiceExplanations.C: ATP synthase conducts protons, not electrons.
choiceExplanations.D: Glucose does not move through Complex IV.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing the direction of respiratory proton pumping with the direction of proton flow through ATP synthase.

questions.stem: If genes E and F assort independently and both are required for normal respiration, what F2 phenotypic ratio is expected after selfing EeFf algae?

choices.text: 1 normal : 1 slow
9 normal : 3 slow : 4 lethal
3 normal : 1 slow
9 normal : 7 slow

questions.explanation: Normal respiration requires at least one functional E allele and one functional F allele, giving E_F_ = 9/16. All other genotypes lack at least one required component and are slow, giving 7/16.

choiceExplanations.A: This is a simple testcross-like ratio, not the expected F2 ratio from EeFf selfing.
choiceExplanations.B: No lethal class is described, and loss of both genes is not more severe than single loss.
choiceExplanations.C: This is a one-gene heterozygote self-cross ratio.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly applies complementary gene action for two independently assorting genes.

questions.commonMistake: Using a standard 9:3:3:1 ratio without combining all genotypes missing either required gene.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the representative strain data in Table p9_t2?

choices.text: Genes E and F are both required for normal Complex IV activity.
Gene E is required for Complex I activity, whereas gene F is not.
The double mutant has much higher respiration than either single mutant.
Loss of either gene increases oxygen consumption by accelerating Complex IV.

questions.explanation: Both eeF_ and E_ff strains have normal Complex I activity but low Complex IV activity and low oxygen consumption. The double mutant is similar, supporting the conclusion that E and F are both needed for normal Complex IV function.

choiceExplanations.A: This matches the low Complex IV activity when either gene is lost.
choiceExplanations.B: Complex I activity remains near normal in all mutant strains.
choiceExplanations.C: The double mutant has low respiration similar to the single mutants.
choiceExplanations.D: Oxygen consumption decreases when either gene is lost.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that any respiratory defect must originate at Complex I rather than using the measured complex activities.

questions.stem: Why is the NADH/NAD+ ratio elevated in strains with low Complex IV activity?

choices.text: Complex IV inhibition increases the amount of oxygen available as a carbon source.
Complex IV directly synthesizes NADH from ATP and oxygen.
Blocked downstream electron transfer slows NADH oxidation, causing upstream carriers to remain more reduced.
Loss of Complex IV forces NADH to become a peptide bond donor.

questions.explanation: Complex IV transfers electrons to oxygen. When Complex IV is deficient, electrons accumulate upstream and NADH oxidation slows, increasing the NADH/NAD+ ratio.

choiceExplanations.A: Oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor, not a carbon source in this context.
choiceExplanations.B: Complex IV does not synthesize NADH from ATP.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly links downstream blockade to reduced upstream carriers.
choiceExplanations.D: NADH does not donate peptide bonds.

questions.commonMistake: Predicting that low oxygen consumption means NADH must be depleted rather than accumulated.

questions.stem: A slow-respiration eeFF strain is crossed with a slow-respiration EEff strain. What phenotype is expected for the diploid EeFf progeny if the mutations affect different genes as described?

choices.text: No growth on fermentable carbon
Slow respiration identical to eeff
Normal respiration
Respiration faster than wild type because both mutations complement excessively

questions.explanation: The cross restores one functional allele at each gene, producing EeFf progeny. Because both E and F functions are present, normal respiration is expected under the complementary gene model.

choiceExplanations.A: The passage states even mutant strains are slow but viable on fermentable carbon.
choiceExplanations.B: EeFf contains functional alleles of both genes and should not resemble eeff.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly predicts complementation between mutations in different genes.
choiceExplanations.D: Complementation restores normal function but does not imply respiration above wild type.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing complementation between different genes with failure to complement alleles of the same gene.

questions.stem: Which additional measurement would best help determine whether the low Complex IV activity in mutants reflects a specific Complex IV assembly defect rather than fewer mitochondria per cell?

choices.text: Normalize Complex IV activity to a mitochondrial marker such as citrate synthase activity or mitochondrial protein content.
Measure colony color under a microscope without assaying respiration.
Remove oxygen from all cultures before measuring Complex IV activity.
Measure only cytosolic ribosome abundance.

questions.explanation: A mitochondrial marker provides a control for mitochondrial abundance. If Complex IV activity remains selectively low after normalization, the result supports a specific assembly or Complex IV functional defect.

choiceExplanations.A: This directly addresses the alternative explanation of reduced mitochondrial mass.
choiceExplanations.B: Colony color is not a direct control for mitochondrial abundance or Complex IV activity.
choiceExplanations.C: Removing oxygen would interfere with respiratory measurement rather than control for mitochondrial content.
choiceExplanations.D: Cytosolic ribosome abundance does not indicate mitochondrial abundance.

questions.commonMistake: Accepting a lower enzyme activity measurement without considering whether there is less organelle material in the sample.

questions.stem: A researcher wants to replace a surface-exposed glutamate with a residue that preserves negative charge at physiological pH but changes side-chain length. Which substitution best fits this goal?

choices.text: Lysine
Aspartate
Phenylalanine
Asparagine

questions.explanation: Glutamate and aspartate are both negatively charged acidic amino acids at physiological pH, but aspartate has a shorter side chain. This makes Asp the best substitution for preserving charge while changing length.

choiceExplanations.A: Lysine is positively charged at physiological pH.
choiceExplanations.B: This preserves negative charge while shortening the side chain.
choiceExplanations.C: Phenylalanine is bulky and hydrophobic rather than negatively charged.
choiceExplanations.D: Asparagine is polar but uncharged at physiological pH.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing an amide residue because its name resembles an acidic residue, despite loss of charge.

questions.stem: A mature eukaryotic mRNA is found in the cytosol with a 5' cap and poly(A) tail but contains no introns. Which process most directly removed the introns?

choices.text: Splicing of the pre-mRNA by spliceosomal machinery
Peptide bond formation by the ribosome
Replication of the DNA template by DNA polymerase
Oxidation of NADH by the electron transport chain

questions.explanation: Eukaryotic pre-mRNAs commonly undergo splicing, in which introns are removed and exons are joined. The 5' cap and poly(A) tail are separate mRNA processing features.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies intron removal as splicing.
choiceExplanations.B: Ribosomes translate mature mRNA but do not remove introns.
choiceExplanations.C: DNA replication copies DNA and does not process mRNA.
choiceExplanations.D: NADH oxidation is metabolic and unrelated to intron removal.

questions.commonMistake: Grouping all mRNA processing events together without distinguishing splicing from capping and polyadenylation.

questions.stem: An enzyme-catalyzed reaction has a negative Delta G under cellular conditions. What does this imply about the enzyme's effect on the reaction?

choices.text: The reaction is thermodynamically favorable, and the enzyme accelerates approach to equilibrium without changing Delta G.
The enzyme makes the reaction favorable by lowering the free energy of products only.
The enzyme changes the equilibrium constant so that only products can exist.
The reaction cannot occur because all enzyme-catalyzed reactions require positive Delta G.

questions.explanation: A negative Delta G means the reaction is thermodynamically favorable as written under the specified conditions. Enzymes lower activation energy and increase reaction rate, but they do not change Delta G or the equilibrium constant.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly separates thermodynamics from catalysis.
choiceExplanations.B: Enzymes do not make reactions favorable by selectively lowering product free energy.
choiceExplanations.C: Enzymes do not alter the equilibrium constant.
choiceExplanations.D: Many enzyme-catalyzed reactions have negative Delta G under cellular conditions.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking enzymes change reaction free energy rather than activation energy.

questions.stem: A heterozygous Aa organism is crossed with an aa organism. If A is completely dominant to a and the locus does not affect viability, what phenotype ratio is expected among offspring?

choices.text: All dominant phenotype
3 dominant phenotype : 1 recessive phenotype
9 dominant phenotype : 7 recessive phenotype
1 dominant phenotype : 1 recessive phenotype

questions.explanation: The Aa parent produces A and a gametes in equal proportions, while the aa parent produces only a gametes. The offspring are 1 Aa and 1 aa, giving a 1:1 phenotype ratio.

choiceExplanations.A: Half the offspring are expected to be aa and show the recessive phenotype.
choiceExplanations.B: A 3:1 ratio is expected from selfing two heterozygotes at one locus.
choiceExplanations.C: A 9:7 ratio is a two-gene complementary interaction pattern, not this single-locus cross.
choiceExplanations.D: This is the expected result of a single-locus testcross.

questions.commonMistake: Using the heterozygote self-cross ratio instead of the testcross ratio.

questions.stem: A mitochondrial preparation is supplied with ADP and phosphate, but the inner mitochondrial membrane is mechanically ruptured. Which outcome is most likely?

choices.text: Electron transfer becomes unnecessary because ADP donates electrons directly to oxygen.
ATP synthesis increases because protons can diffuse freely to ATP synthase.
ATP synthesis decreases because the proton gradient cannot be maintained.
The mitochondrial DNA code changes to compensate for membrane rupture.

questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation requires an intact inner mitochondrial membrane to maintain the proton-motive force. If the membrane is ruptured, protons cannot be compartmentalized effectively, so ATP synthesis by ATP synthase decreases.

choiceExplanations.A: ADP does not donate electrons to oxygen in the electron transport chain.
choiceExplanations.B: Free proton diffusion dissipates the gradient rather than improving ATP synthesis.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly identifies loss of the proton gradient as the key consequence.
choiceExplanations.D: Membrane damage does not alter the genetic code.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking proton access alone is sufficient for ATP synthesis, rather than recognizing the need for a gradient.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the effect of PC inhibitor C in Table p10_t1?

choices.text: MPC import is accelerated, preventing cytosolic pyruvate accumulation.
PEPCK activity is increased, causing oxaloacetate to accumulate.
Pyruvate enters or accumulates, but conversion to mitochondrial oxaloacetate is impaired.
Gluconeogenesis increases because PC normally inhibits glucose output.

questions.explanation: PC inhibitor C causes high cytosolic pyruvate, low mitochondrial OAA/malate pool, and very low glucose output. This supports a block at pyruvate carboxylase, which normally converts mitochondrial pyruvate to oxaloacetate.

choiceExplanations.A: Cytosolic pyruvate increases, not decreases, with PC inhibitor C.
choiceExplanations.B: PEPCK knockdown, not PC inhibition, causes OAA/malate accumulation in the table.
choiceExplanations.C: This matches the accumulation of pyruvate with depletion of downstream OAA/malate.
choiceExplanations.D: Glucose output falls, showing PC supports rather than inhibits gluconeogenesis in this context.

questions.commonMistake: Interpreting pyruvate accumulation as evidence of increased pathway flux rather than a downstream block.

questions.stem: Which finding most strongly distinguishes PEPCK knockdown from PC inhibition?

choices.text: PEPCK knockdown eliminates cytosolic pyruvate, whereas PC inhibition has no effect on pyruvate.
PEPCK knockdown increases glucose output, whereas PC inhibition lowers glucose output.
PEPCK knockdown causes accumulation of the mitochondrial OAA/malate pool, whereas PC inhibition lowers it.
PEPCK knockdown raises ATP/ADP to a higher value than control.

questions.explanation: PC acts before oxaloacetate formation, so PC inhibition lowers the OAA/malate pool. PEPCK acts after oxaloacetate formation, so PEPCK knockdown causes OAA/malate accumulation while still lowering glucose output.

choiceExplanations.A: Both conditions show elevated cytosolic pyruvate relative to control.
choiceExplanations.B: Both perturbations lower glucose output.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly uses the opposite OAA/malate pool changes to localize the blocks.
choiceExplanations.D: ATP/ADP is lower than control with PEPCK knockdown.

questions.commonMistake: Using glucose output alone, even though multiple different blocks can reduce glucose output.

questions.stem: Why does the low-dose ETC inhibitor reduce glucose output in HGL cells?

choices.text: The ETC inhibitor directly removes all lactate and alanine from the medium.
Reduced oxidative phosphorylation lowers ATP availability for energy-requiring gluconeogenic steps.
The ETC inhibitor converts glucose into mitochondrial DNA.
Reduced O2 consumption necessarily means PC becomes more active than control.

questions.explanation: The passage states that gluconeogenesis requires substantial energy input. The ETC inhibitor lowers O2 consumption and ATP/ADP ratio, so reduced ATP availability provides a plausible explanation for lower glucose output.

choiceExplanations.A: No passage data indicate removal of lactate or alanine from the medium.
choiceExplanations.B: This integrates the lower O2 consumption, lower ATP/ADP ratio, and energy requirement of gluconeogenesis.
choiceExplanations.C: Glucose is not converted into mitochondrial DNA.
choiceExplanations.D: The ETC inhibitor lowers the OAA/malate pool and glucose output; increased PC activity is not supported.

questions.commonMistake: Treating gluconeogenesis as energy-neutral and ignoring the ATP/ADP measurement.

questions.stem: The acetyl-CoA analog increases glucose output and the mitochondrial OAA/malate pool. Which mechanism is most consistent with this observation?

choices.text: Allosteric activation of pyruvate carboxylase increases conversion of pyruvate to oxaloacetate.
Direct inhibition of MPC prevents pyruvate from reaching mitochondria.
Destruction of ATP prevents gluconeogenesis from consuming energy.
Conversion of oxaloacetate into ribosomal RNA increases translation.

questions.explanation: The acetyl-CoA analog lowers cytosolic pyruvate, raises the mitochondrial OAA/malate pool, and increases glucose output. This pattern fits activation of PC, which converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly explains the direction of pyruvate, OAA/malate, and glucose-output changes.
choiceExplanations.B: MPC inhibition produces the opposite pattern of high cytosolic pyruvate and low OAA/malate.
choiceExplanations.C: The ATP/ADP ratio remains high enough and glucose output rises, not falls.
choiceExplanations.D: Oxaloacetate is a metabolic intermediate, not a direct precursor to ribosomal RNA in the described pathway.

questions.commonMistake: Calling any metabolite analog an inhibitor without using the measured direction of pathway flux.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best distinguish whether inhibitor B primarily blocks mitochondrial pyruvate import rather than directly inhibiting PC catalysis?

choices.text: Measure radiolabeled pyruvate uptake into isolated mitochondria and separately assay PC activity in disrupted mitochondrial extracts supplied with pyruvate.
Measure only total glucose output in intact cells after adding inhibitor B.
Sequence ribosomal RNA to determine whether pyruvate codons changed.
Add a Complex IV inhibitor and assume any further decrease identifies the MPC target.

questions.explanation: Directly measuring mitochondrial pyruvate uptake tests the transport hypothesis, while measuring PC activity in disrupted extracts bypasses membrane import and tests whether PC catalysis itself is inhibited. Together, these assays distinguish transporter inhibition from enzyme inhibition.

choiceExplanations.A: This design separately tests pyruvate import and PC catalytic activity.
choiceExplanations.B: Total glucose output alone cannot localize the inhibited step.
choiceExplanations.C: Ribosomal RNA sequence is unrelated to pyruvate import or PC catalysis.
choiceExplanations.D: Adding an ETC inhibitor would introduce an energy confound and would not identify the primary target of inhibitor B.

questions.commonMistake: Using a whole-pathway endpoint when the mechanistic alternatives require separate transport and enzyme assays.
